United States.text:
As tournament hosts ranked 16th in the world, the United States carry the automatic boost of home advantage, and the model reflects it: a 47.5% chance to win their group and an 87% chance to clear the round of 32. From there the curve steepens — 56% to reach the last 16, 26.9% to the quarter-finals, and just 1.6% to lift the trophy on home soil. With a squad valued at €385.65m, this is a roster built to go deep, but the model treats anything beyond the quarters as a genuine long shot.

The recent form is the worry. The last 10 tell a split story: comfortable home wins over Uruguay (5-1), Japan and Paraguay sit alongside heavy defeats to Belgium (2-5), Portugal (0-2) and, most recently, a 1-2 loss to Germany in June. That run against elite European opposition is a flashing caution light heading into the finals. The talent is there across a settled defensive spine of Ream, Richards and McKenzie, but if the USMNT are to validate that 56% knockout figure, the results against the better sides have to start matching the friendlies they win at a canter.

Canada.text:
As one of the three hosts, Canada arrives ranked 30th in the world with a squad valued at €198.65m, and the model treats home advantage seriously: it makes them favourites to win their group at 56.75% and gives them a 97.74% chance of clearing the opening round into the last 32. From there the path narrows — 63.1% to reach the round of 16, 27.05% to the quarter-finals, and just 8.68% to a semi. The dream of lifting the trophy sits at 0.73%, which is roughly what you'd expect of a solid but not elite side carried by familiar faces.

The recent form reads quietly. Across their last ten, all but one at home, Canada won four, drew five and lost only once, a 0-1 to Australia last October. The defensive shape looks dependable — six clean sheets in that run, including goalless draws with Colombia, Ecuador and Tunisia — but the goals have been scarce, with five straight matches yielding two or fewer. A back line built around Johnston, Laryea and Cornelius keeps them in games; whether they can score enough to justify that 27% quarter-final number is the open question.

Mexico.text:
Mexico arrive as hosts ranked 15th in the world, and the recent form backs the billing: their last ten have produced seven wins, including a 5-1 dismantling of Serbia and a 4-0 over Iceland, with goalless draws against Portugal and Uruguay showing the defence can hold its shape against quality. The only blemish is a 1-2 home loss to Paraguay back in November. With a squad valued at €191.85m and three experienced goalkeepers led by veteran G. Ochoa, this is a settled, home-comfortable side carrying genuine momentum into the tournament.

The model likes the platform but not the ceiling. It makes Mexico clear favourites to win their group at 57.96% and gives them a 56.88% chance of reaching the round of 16, but the numbers thin quickly after that: 23.7% to make the quarter-finals, 7.85% to the semis, and just 0.74% to lift the trophy. That gap is the honest story here — host advantage and form should carry Mexico into the knockouts comfortably, but the model sees them as a team likelier to be a quarter-final wall than a champion, and the deep run remains very much an open question.

Panama.text:
Panama arrive ranked 33rd in the world, a CONCACAF side whose qualifying form was solid rather than spectacular: a 3-0 win over El Salvador and a 3-2 result away to Guatemala in November, either side of a 1-1 home draw with Suriname. The friendly run since has been mixed — a 4-2 win over the Dominican Republic and a 2-1 victory away to South Africa sit alongside a heavy 6-2 loss in Brazil and a 1-1 draw with Bosnia & Herzegovina. That's a team that can score against most opponents but has shown it can leak goals badly against elite attacks.

The model treats this as a tournament about survival, not silverware. Panama are given a 28.3% chance of reaching the round of 32 and just 3.79% of making the round of 16, with the quarter-finals (0.51%) effectively out of reach and a 1.67% shot at winning their group. With a squad valued at €34.55m, those are honest numbers for a side that will likely need a result against stronger opposition to extend its stay. Getting out of the group would already count as a real success.

Argentina.text:
The defending champions arrive ranked third in the world and carrying the form to match: nine wins in their last ten, the only blemish a 0-1 loss away to Ecuador last September, with friendly routs of Zambia (5-0) and Puerto Rico (6-0) bookending the run. The squad is valued at €782.5m and anchored by a goalkeeping department deep enough to list Emiliano Martínez, Musso and Rulli, with Lisandro Martínez marshalling a back line. This is a team that has reached six World Cup finals and lifted the trophy in 1978, 1986 and 2022, and it is built to do damage again.

Our model makes Argentina the single most likely champion at 19.1%, and gives them a 28.8% shot at reaching the final — comfortably the strongest figures in the field. The path looks orderly: a 76.4% chance of winning their group and a 99.1% likelihood of clearing the round of 32. The numbers tighten from there, with reaching the semi-finals priced at 43.8%, a reminder that even the favourites are closer to coin-flips than coronations once the knockouts narrow.

Brazil.text:
Brazil arrive ranked sixth in the world with the most valuable squad in the field at €928.2m, and the model treats them accordingly: a 61.2% chance of winning their group and a 10.4% shot at lifting the trophy, the kind of number that marks them as a genuine contender without making them favourites. The path tells the story — 98.2% to clear the round of 32, 68.7% to reach the last 16, but only 31.3% to make a semi-final and 18.1% to reach the final. The deeper you go, the thinner the margin.

The recent form is harder to read than the price tag suggests. The headline results are emphatic — 6-2 over Panama, 5-0 away to South Korea — but mixed in are a 0-1 qualifying loss in Bolivia, a 2-3 defeat in Japan and a 1-2 home loss to France. Against the better sides, A seleção have looked beatable. With Alisson, Ederson and Weverton behind a back line built around Gabriel Magalhães and Bremer, the foundation is strong; the question the model keeps asking is whether it holds against elite opposition.

Colombia.text:
Colombia arrive ranked 13th in the world and carrying real momentum, with squad value around €302m and a recent run that says plenty. Their last ten games include a 6-3 win away to Venezuela in qualifying, a 4-0 drubbing of Mexico in Mexico, and warm-up wins over Jordan and Costa Rica. The only blemishes came against serious European opposition, back-to-back home defeats to Croatia (1-2) and France (1-3) in March, a reminder that the step up in class still bites. Los Cafeteros otherwise look hard to beat.

The model treats them as a solid but not elite side. It gives Colombia a 34.9% chance of winning their group and an 86.9% shot at reaching the round of 32, so survival into the knockouts is close to a formality. From there the odds thin out fast: 56.3% to make the last 16, 29.6% to reach the quarter-finals, and just 14.6% to play a semi. A run to the final sits at 6.7%, and the title at 2.8%, putting them firmly among the contenders without ever being favourites.

Uruguay.text:
Marcelo Bielsa's Uruguay arrive ranked 17th in the world, with a squad valued at €359.3m, but the recent evidence is mixed. Qualifying produced a tidy 3-0 win over Peru and a 2-0 home defeat of Venezuela, alongside a goalless draw in Chile and a 0-2 loss in Paraguay. The friendly schedule since has been harder to read: a creditable 1-1 in England and a 0-0 in Algeria sit awkwardly next to a 1-5 thrashing by the USA and another blank draw against Mexico. For a side built on defensive names like Araújo, Giménez and Olivera, the goals have dried up at both ends.

The model treats them as solid rather than spectacular. Reaching the round of 32 is close to a formality at 90.7%, but winning the group is only a 22.3% shot, and the survival rate falls steeply thereafter — 36.7% to make the last 16, 20.9% to the quarters, and just 10.5% to a semi-final. A 1.75% title chance frames Uruguay as a credible knockout team whose ceiling depends on rediscovering the cutting edge those recent draws have lacked.

Ecuador.text:
Ecuador arrive unbeaten across their last ten matches, a run built on a defence that rarely cracks: 0-0 draws away to Canada and Paraguay, 1-1 stalemates against the Netherlands, Morocco, Mexico and the USA, and a 1-0 home win over Argentina in qualifying. La Tricolor are 23rd in the FIFA ranking, and a squad valued at €368.7m leans heavily on its back line, with the likes of Pacho, Hincapié, Estupiñán and Ordoñez giving Ecuador a hard, organised spine. The friendly form has tilted brighter lately, with 3-0 and 2-1 home wins over Guatemala and Saudi Arabia.

The model treats them as a solid knockout side rather than a contender. It makes them 89.7% to clear the group stage and 48.7% to reach the last 16, with a 28.1% shot at topping their group. Beyond that the odds thin quickly: 21.7% to make the quarter-finals, 9.6% for the semis and just 1.4% to lift the trophy. That profile fits the evidence — a team that suffocates opponents and grinds out results, but whose draw-heavy run hints at how often the goals dry up.

Paraguay.text:
Paraguay arrive ranked 40th in the world, a side whose pedigree — two Copa América titles, ten World Cups and a quarter-final run in 2010 — outstrips a modest squad valued at €153.65m. The qualifying campaign that brought them here was built on grit rather than goals: a 1-0 win in Peru, a goalless draw with Ecuador and a narrow loss in Brazil tell the story of a hard-to-beat team that keeps games tight. Recent friendlies have been mixed, a 4-0 dismantling of Nicaragua and an away win over Mexico sitting alongside defeats to Morocco, the USA and South Korea, so the form line is genuinely uneven heading into the tournament.

The model treats them as a credible but unspectacular outsider. It gives Paraguay a 52.9% chance of surviving the group stage and reaching the round of 32, but only a 12.1% shot at winning the group and a 20.6% chance of progressing to the last 16. From there the odds thin sharply — 5.5% to reach the quarter-finals, and just 0.07% to lift the trophy. A solid floor, in other words, with a low ceiling.

Germany.text:
Germany arrive in form, having won nine of their last ten, the only blemish a 0-2 defeat to Slovakia back in September 2025 — a result they avenged emphatically with a 6-0 home win in November. Friendly results since have been encouraging if not airtight: 4-0 over Finland, 2-1 away to the USA, but also a 4-3 win in Switzerland that hints at defensive lapses. The squad is valued at €947m and ranks 10th in FIFA's order, and the defensive group around Rüdiger and Schlotterbeck, with Neuer among the goalkeeping options, gives the spine a familiar look.

The model makes them roughly a coin-flip to win their group at 48.9%, and a near-certainty to clear the first knockout round, with a 96.1% chance of reaching the round of 32 and 61.4% of making the last 16. From there it thins quickly: 30.3% to reach the quarters, 15.2% the semis, and just 2.9% to lift the trophy. That last number frames the honest read — a strong, well-drilled side, but not among the clear favourites.

England.text:
England arrive ranked fourth in the world with a squad valued at €1.36bn, and the qualifying campaign backs up that billing: five wins from five in European qualification, including 5-0 routs of Latvia and Serbia, with clean sheets in every one. The model is duly impressed, making England 74% to win their group and an overwhelming 98.7% to clear the first knockout round. Champion sits at 10.5%, with reaching the final priced at 18% — respectable, but a reminder that even a top-four side is more likely than not to fall short of the last two.

The caveat is the friendly form. Since qualifying wrapped up, England have looked ordinary: a 1-1 draw with Uruguay, a 0-1 home loss to Japan and a narrow 1-0 over New Zealand, on top of a 1-3 defeat to Senegal last June. The model's confidence rests heavily on the competitive results, not these warm-ups, so the gap between a ruthless qualifier and a stuttering friendly side is the real open question. Get past the quarter-final, which the model rates at 51%, and the deeper run becomes plausible.

France.text:
France arrive as the world's number one side, and the model treats them with respect without crowning them: an 8% title chance, a 15.5% shot at the final and a 56.4% read on winning their group. The squad's €1,520m value underlines the depth, anchored by Maignan in goal and a defence stacked with Konaté and both Hernández brothers. Reaching the last 32 is close to a formality at 95.8%, and the model has them better than even money to make the quarter-finals at 45.9%, but the gap between that and the 8% champion figure is the familiar story of a tournament: getting deep is likely, going all the way is not.

The form line is solid rather than flawless. France cruised through European qualifying, beating Ukraine 4-0 and 2-0 and dropping only a 2-2 draw at Iceland. Spring friendlies away to Brazil (2-1) and Colombia (3-1) showed they can win on the road against quality, though a 1-2 home loss to Ivory Coast in June is a reminder that the floor is not bolted shut. The 3-1 send-off against Northern Ireland steadied things.

Spain.text:
Spain arrive ranked second in the world with a squad valued at €1.22bn, and the form to match the billing. Their World Cup qualifying campaign was close to ruthless — a 6-0 away win over Türkiye, four-goal hauls against Georgia and Bulgaria, and only a 2-2 home draw with Türkiye to blot the record. The friendlies since have been flatter: a goalless stalemate with Egypt and a 1-1 draw with Iraq sit alongside a 3-0 dismissal of Serbia and a 3-1 win in Peru. The attacking ceiling is obvious; the question is whether the recent draws are noise or a sign of a side coasting between competitive fixtures.

The model treats Spain as genuine contenders rather than favourites. They are given a 75% chance of winning their group and a near-certain 99.2% to clear the round of 32, but the path narrows quickly: 37.6% to reach the semi-finals, 24.5% to make the final, and 14.4% to lift the trophy. That last figure makes them one of the strongest picks in the field without being a runaway, a reflection of both their quality and the depth of competition ahead.

Portugal.text:
Roberto Martínez's Portugal arrive ranked fifth in the world with a squad valued at just over a billion euros, and the model treats them as genuine contenders without quite believing in a coronation. It gives them a 53.9% chance of winning their group, a 69% shot at reaching the last 16 and a 43.9% chance of making the quarter-finals, but only a 6.5% chance of lifting the trophy — respectable, yet some way behind the favourites. The pieces are there, from Diogo Costa in goal to a defence built around Rúben Dias, João Cancelo and Diogo Dalot, all marshalled by captain Cristiano Ronaldo.

The recent form reads well on balance but carries a warning. Portugal won six of their last ten and battered Armenia 9-1, yet that run also includes a 0-2 qualifying defeat away to the Republic of Ireland and draws against Hungary and Mexico. That mix of demolitions and stumbles is why the model stops short of the top bracket: the 24% semi-final and 12.8% final figures say a deep run is plausible, not expected. Treat them as a strong side with a ceiling, not a certainty.

Netherlands.text:
The Netherlands arrive ranked seventh in the world with a squad valued at €754.2m, but the form line is more mixed than that pedigree suggests. Qualifying was largely comfortable — four 4-0 wins among them, against Lithuania, Finland and Malta — yet the Dutch twice drew 1-1 with Poland and never fully pulled clear. The recent friendlies underline the wobble: a 2-1 win over Uzbekistan and a 2-1 defeat of Norway sit either side of a 0-1 home loss to Algeria and a 1-1 draw with Ecuador. A defence built around Virgil van Dijk, Nathan Aké, Denzel Dumfries and Jurriën Timber should be the backbone, with three goalkeepers in Flekken, Roefs and Verbruggen competing for the gloves.

The model likes the floor more than the ceiling. It makes the Netherlands 89.9% to clear the group of 32 and 43% to win their group outright, but the path narrows fast — 41.6% to reach the last 16, 25.1% for the quarters and just 12.6% to make a semi-final. At 2.4% to lift the trophy, they are a credible dark horse rather than a favourite.

Belgium.text:
Belgium arrive ranked ninth in the world with a squad valued at €547.5m, and the recent form backs the billing. Their qualifying campaign was largely a goal-fest — 7-0 over Liechtenstein, 6-0 twice over Kazakhstan and Liechtenstein again, 4-2 at Wales — though the draws at home to FYR Macedonia and away in Kazakhstan are a reminder that the floor isn't quite as high as the ceiling. Warm-up wins have continued the trend: 5-0 against Tunisia, 2-0 at Croatia, 5-2 at the USA. With Thibaut Courtois behind a back line of Castagne, Debast and De Winter, this is a team that scores in bursts.

The model likes them to top their group at 63% and rates them clear favourites to reach the round of 32 at 95%. From there the path narrows in the usual way: a 66% chance of the last 16, 36% for the quarter-finals, and just under 16% to reach a semi. The title number sits at 2.8%, enough to count them among the contenders without making them favourites. Belgium look a safe bet to go deep, less so to go all the way.

Austria.text:
Austria arrive in good order. Eight wins in their last ten, a single defeat — 0-1 away to Romania in October — and three friendly victories this spring, including a 5-1 dismantling of Ghana and clean-sheet wins over South Korea and Tunisia. The qualifying run leaned heavily on a meanly drilled defence: 1-0 wins over Cyprus, a 2-1 away victory in Bosnia and two scorelines against San Marino, 10-0 and 4-0, that flatter the goal column. Ranked 24th in the world, with a squad valued at €242.2m and David Alaba anchoring a back line that also features Danso, Friedl and Lienhart, this is a team built to be hard to beat rather than to dazzle.

The model sees a side likely to advance but unlikely to linger. Austria are given a 70.5% chance of clearing the group stage and reaching the round of 32, yet only 8% to win their group and 21.1% to make the last 16. From there the odds thin quickly — 8.3% to reach the quarter-finals, 2.6% to the semis, and just 0.16% to lift the trophy. A solid floor, a modest ceiling.

Switzerland.text:
Switzerland arrive ranked 19th in the world, and the route here was solid: their European qualifying campaign produced wins over Sweden home and away (4-1 and 2-0), Slovenia and Kosovo, with only draws at Slovenia and Kosovo to blemish it. The friendly form since is murkier — a 4-1 dismissal of Jordan, but also a 1-1 against Australia, a goalless draw in Norway, and a 4-3 home loss to Germany that exposed the back line. With a squad valued at €332.5m and led by defender Manuel Akanji, this is a side built to grind rather than overwhelm.

The model treats them as a near-lock to escape the group stage at 95.5% to reach the round of 32, and rates them roughly even money to make the last 16 at 55.3%. From there the air thins fast: a 20% shot at the quarter-finals, 6.9% at the semis, and just 0.63% to lift the trophy. That feels about right for a team good enough to trouble anyone on its day but, on the Germany evidence, leaky enough to be undone by it too.

Turkey.text:
Turkey arrive in good rhythm, winning seven of their last ten and carrying a run of six straight victories into the tournament, including tight 1-0 qualifying wins over Romania and Kosovo and a 4-0 friendly rout of FYR Macedonia. The blot on the record is Spain, who took both honours emphatically: a 6-0 humbling in Seville's reverse fixture sense back in September, softened only by a 2-2 draw away in November. Ranked 22nd by FIFA with a squad valued at €473.7m, this is a side that beats everyone below the elite and has yet to prove it can live with the very top.

The model reads them as a solid knockout side rather than a contender. It gives Turkey a 64.8% chance to clear the group stage and a 30.1% shot at the last 16, but only 19.4% to win their group outright. From there the odds thin quickly: 9.6% to reach the quarter-finals, 2.9% for the semis, and just 0.2% to lift the trophy. A deep run is plausible; anything beyond the quarters would be a genuine overachievement.

Croatia.text:
Croatia arrive ranked 11th in the world, their qualifying campaign a model of efficiency: seven wins and a goalless draw away to the Czech Republic, with Montenegro beaten home and away and Faroe Islands twice seen off. The squad, valued at €387.3m, leans on a settled defensive spine in Josko Gvardiol, Josip Stanisic and Dejan Lovren's heirs at the back, plus a goalkeeping rotation of Livakovic, Kotarski and Pandur. The recent friendlies told a more sobering story — a 0-2 home loss to Belgium and a 1-3 defeat in Brazil sandwiching narrow wins over Colombia and Slovenia — a reminder that the step up from qualifying minnows to genuine contenders is steep.

Our model reads them as a solid knockout side rather than a title threat. They reach the round of 32 in 87% of simulations and clear the round of 16 in 39%, but the projection thins fast: a 14.5% quarter-final, 5.6% semi-final and just a 0.5% chance of lifting the trophy. Winning the group sits at 20.6%, so a tougher draw and an early test look the likeliest path through.

Scotland.text:
Scotland arrive ranked 43rd in the world, qualifying through a European campaign that ended on a high: a 4-2 win over Denmark and a 3-1 home result against Greece bookended a run that included a goalless draw away in Copenhagen and two wins over Belarus. The form since has been streaky — back-to-back 1-0 home defeats to Japan and Ivory Coast in March, then 4-0 and 4-1 friendly routs of Bolivia and Curaçao. A squad valued at €170.25m leans on experience, with three goalkeepers in Gordon, Gunn and Kelly and a defensive core built around Hanley, Hendry, Hickey, Hyam and McKenna.

The model is sober about the ceiling. It gives Scotland a 42.5% chance of reaching the round of 32 but only 10.6% to make the last 16, and just 2.8% to win their group outright. Beyond that the odds thin out fast — 2.5% to reach a quarter-final, 0.5% a semi, and a rounding-error 0.01% to lift the trophy. The realistic question here is survival past the group stage, where the numbers say it's closer to a coin-toss-and-a-half against.

Morocco.text:
Morocco arrive ranked eighth in the world and carrying real momentum. They lifted the African Cup of Nations earlier this year on the back of a clean knockout run — beating Zambia, Cameroon, Nigeria and Senegal without conceding much of note — and the friendly results since have followed the same line, with 4-0 and 5-0 wins over Madagascar and Burundi either side of a 1-1 draw with Norway. A squad valued at €498.3m and built around familiar names like Bounou, Aguerd and Hakimi gives the Atlas Lions both depth and a settled spine.

The model likes them without crowning them. It makes Morocco 94.4% to clear the group stage and 34.5% to win the group outright, with a 56.7% chance of reaching the last 16. From there the odds thin in the usual way — 36.5% to make the quarter-finals, 20.3% for the semis, and 4.8% to go all the way. That champion number puts them in the second tier of contenders rather than the favourites, which feels about right for a side that does the hard defensive work well but still has to prove it over seven games.

Senegal.text:
Senegal arrive ranked 14th in the world and carrying a squad worth €478.1m, and the Lions de la Teranga's recent form looks the part. They reached the Africa Cup of Nations on a run that included shutout wins over Botswana and Benin and a 1-0 edging of Egypt, before Morocco exposed them 3-0. A 2-3 friendly loss to the USA in late May is the kind of result that tempers expectations, even if friendlies tell you less than the competitive games either side of them. Koulibaly anchors a defensive spine that kept three clean sheets across that AFCON run, and goalkeeping depth — Mendy, Diaw and Diouf — is not a worry.

The model treats them as solid rather than spectacular. They're given a 83.5% chance to clear the group stage and a 46.8% shot at the round of 16, but the odds thin quickly after that: 21.6% to reach the quarters, 9.5% for the semis, and just 1.4% to lift the trophy. Winning their group sits at 22.2%, so a runners-up route looks the likelier path through.

Egypt.text:
Egypt arrive ranked 29th in the world and valued at a modest €116.48m, and the model treats them as a side likely to clear the first hurdle but little more: a 68.0% chance of reaching the round of 32 sits alongside just 27.3% to make the round of 16 and 7.5% to reach the quarter-finals. Winning their group is rated at 15.3%, and the deep runs are remote — 2.0% to a semi-final, 0.1% to lift the trophy. This is a team the numbers see surviving the group stage more often than not, then running out of road.

The recent form reads as genuinely split. The Pharaohs flattered to deceive at the Africa Cup of Nations, opening with wins over South Africa, Benin and Ivory Coast before falling to Senegal and Nigeria to bow out. The friendly slate since has been more encouraging: a 4-0 win in Saudi Arabia, a goalless draw away to Spain, and a 1-0 home win over Russia, before a narrow 1-2 loss to Brazil. Encouraging signs against strong opposition, but nothing yet that demands the model revise upward.

Ivory Coast.text:
The Elephants arrive in form. Champions of Africa for the third time in 2023, Ivory Coast have turned that momentum into a friendly run that catches the eye: a 4-0 win over South Korea, a 1-0 win at Scotland, and most strikingly a 2-1 victory away to France in June. That is the kind of result that travels well into a World Cup, the fourth qualification in the nation's history and the first since 2014. A squad valued at €522.1m and ranked 34th in the world is built on a deep, well-stocked defence, from Kossounou and Diomande to the Doué and Konan names familiar across Europe.

The model is respectful rather than reverent. It makes Ivory Coast comfortable to clear the round of 32 at 85.9% and roughly a coin-flip to reach the last 16 at 41.6%, with a 22.4% chance of winning the group outright. Beyond that the odds thin quickly: 16.4% to reach the quarter-finals and just 0.7% to lift the trophy. A January loss to Egypt at AFCON is the reminder that the ceiling and the floor still sit some distance apart.

South Africa.text:
Bafana Bafana arrive ranked 60th in the world and carry one of the leaner squads in the field, valued at just €49.25m, and the recent form does little to suggest a side about to punch above that. Their last ten tell a story of stalemates and narrow margins: draws against Jamaica, Nicaragua and twice with Panama, a group-stage Africa Cup of Nations run that ended in defeats to Egypt and Cameroon around wins over Angola and Zimbabwe. The goals have dried up at the wrong moments, and South Africa look more like a side that grinds out 1-1s than one that breaks games open.

The model reads it the same way. It gives South Africa a 33.97% chance of reaching the round of 32 — better than a coin flip against, but hardly comfortable — and only a 4.23% shot at topping their group. Beyond that the door shuts quickly: 7.33% to make the round of 16, 1.07% to reach a quarter-final, and effectively nothing past that. This is a tournament about survival rather than ambition, and even survival is no better than an even-money bet.

DR Congo.text:
The Leopards arrive ranked 46th, having booked their place through the intercontinental play-offs with a 1-0 win over Jamaica, and they got there on a genuinely solid run. Across their last ten they've lost only once — 0-1 to Algeria at the Africa Cup of Nations — while holding Denmark scoreless, beating Botswana 3-0 and edging Benin and Cameroon away. A squad worth €143.9m, anchored by a deep goalkeeping group and defenders like Kalulu, Batubinsika and Masuaku, looks built to frustrate opponents, which fits the low-scoring, clean-sheet pattern in those recent results.

The model is more cautious than the form suggests. It gives DR Congo a 40.9% chance of escaping the group into the round of 32, but only 12.4% to reach the round of 16 and 3.3% to make the quarter-finals. Winning their group is rated at just 5.2%, and the path beyond thins quickly — 0.85% to a semi-final, 0.04% to lift the trophy. In short, this is a side the numbers expect to be competitive for a place in the knockouts, but a clear underdog to go deep once there.

Tunisia.text:
The Eagles of Carthage arrive ranked 44th in the world, and the recent form makes for sobering reading. The warm-up window went badly: a 0-5 thumping in Belgium and a 0-1 loss in Austria followed a goalless draw with Canada, while the Africa Cup of Nations brought just one win in four, group-stage defeats to Nigeria and a draw with lowly Tanzania among them. A squad valued at €69.95m, light on attacking firepower and built around goalkeepers and defenders in the listed names, has scored sparingly against decent opposition.

The model reflects that caution. It gives Tunisia a 38.9% chance of surviving the expanded group stage into the round of 32, but only an 8.9% shot at the round of 16 and a 5.2% chance of actually winning their group. Beyond that the numbers thin out fast — 2.4% to reach the quarter-finals, and effectively nothing, 0.02%, to lift the trophy. This is a side the model sees as competitive for a knockout berth but no further, and the pre-tournament results do little to argue otherwise.

Japan.text:
Japan arrive in form that demands respect. Six straight wins close out the run, and the away results carry real weight: 1-0 at England, 1-0 at Scotland, a 1-0 over Iceland at home and, going back to October, a 3-2 win over Brazil. The blips are honest enough — a 0-0 in Mexico and a 0-2 loss in the USA — but the recent trend is firmly upward. Ranked 18th and carrying a squad valued at €270.85m, the Samurai Blue look like a side that travels well and defends from the front.

The model likes them as a knockout-round fixture rather than a contender. It gives Japan a 90.3% chance of clearing the round of 32 and a 43.4% shot at topping their group, but the odds thin quickly after that: 26.6% to reach the quarter-finals, 13.7% to the semis, and just 2.9% to lift the trophy. That reads as a team good enough to win games it is expected to win and to spring the odd upset, without the depth the numbers see in the genuine favourites. A clean run-out and a kind draw could push them further.

South Korea.text:
South Korea arrive 25th in the world and carry a squad valued at €139m, and the model treats them as a side likely to survive but unlikely to go deep. The numbers tell a consistent story: 80.8% to clear the round of 32, 36.5% to reach the round of 16, but only 11.2% to make the quarter-finals and 0.22% to lift the trophy. Winning the group is rated at 26.1%, so an early knockout exit looks like the base case rather than a disappointment.

The form line explains the caution. Korea have beaten the teams they should — 5-0 over Trinidad and Tobago, 2-0 wins away at the USA and over Paraguay, and recent 1-0 results against El Salvador and Ghana — but they have been outclassed when the level rises, losing 0-5 to Brazil and 0-4 to Ivory Coast at home and 0-1 to Austria away. That gap between comfortable wins over modest opponents and heavy defeats to stronger ones is exactly why the model has them clearing the first hurdle but stalling soon after. Kim Min-Jae anchors a defence that will need to hold up far better than those scorelines suggest.

Iran.text:
Iran arrive ranked 21st in the world and warming up nicely: back-to-back home friendly wins over Mali (2-0) and Gambia (3-1) followed a 5-0 demolition of Costa Rica away in March, so the goals have been flowing of late. The longer view is patchier — a 0-1 home loss to Uzbekistan in the CAFA Nations Cup, a 2-2 draw in Tajikistan and defeats to Nigeria and Russia all sit inside the last ten — but the trend heading into the tournament points upward. This is a squad built on familiar names, with a deep goalkeeping group in Beiranvand, Hosseini and Niazmand behind a defence anchored by veterans like Hajisafi and Rezaeian.

The model treats Iran as a likely qualifier rather than a contender. It gives them a 66.6% chance of surviving the group stage and a 25.7% shot at the round of 16, but those numbers thin quickly thereafter: 6.8% to reach the quarter-finals, 1.6% a semi, and just 0.08% to lift the trophy. With a squad valued at €32.05m, that ceiling is no surprise — progress, not a deep run, is the realistic ambition.

Australia.text:
Australia arrive ranked 27th with one of the lighter squads in the field at €77.45m, and a recent run that swings between encouraging and flat. The FIFA Series in March was the high point — a 1-0 win over Cameroon followed by a 5-1 rout of Curaçao — but either side of that the friendlies tell a harder story: 0-3 in Colombia, 0-1 to Venezuela, 0-1 to Mexico, and a 1-1 draw with Switzerland in June. The recent wins over New Zealand and Canada keep the picture mixed rather than alarming, but the goals have largely dried up against stronger opposition.

The model reads them as a side likely to reach the knockouts and unlikely to go much further. It gives Australia a 68% chance of clearing the group stage and a 21% shot at winning the group, but the odds taper quickly after that — 34% to reach the last 16, 11% to the quarter-finals, and just 3.7% to a semi-final. Champion at 0.3% is a number to admire rather than back. The route is plausible; the ceiling looks firmly set.

Saudi Arabia.text:
Saudi Arabia arrive ranked 61st in the world with a squad valued at just €40.68m, among the lighter price tags in the field, and the recent form sheet reads like a warning. The Arab Cup brought wins over Oman, Comoros and Palestine but defeats to Morocco, Jordan and a heavy 0-4 home loss to Egypt, and the friendly run since has been bumpy too — beaten by Serbia and Ecuador before a 3-0 win over Puerto Rico in June steadied things. That is roughly even results spread across opponents of wildly different quality, which is hard to read cleanly.

The model is blunt about the ceiling. It gives Al-Suqour Al-Arabiyyah a 30.7% chance of escaping the group stage, just a 1.3% shot at winning the group, and effectively no realistic path deeper — 0.8% to reach the quarter-finals and a championship probability that rounds to zero. The squad leans heavily on goalkeeping cover, with Nawaf Al Aqidi, Mohammed Al Owais and Ahmed Al Kassar all named, but the numbers frame this as a side fighting to survive the opening round rather than threaten beyond it.

Uzbekistan.text:
Uzbekistan arrive at their first World Cup ranked 50th in the world and carrying a squad valued at a modest €85.33m, the kind of profile that makes them clear underdogs rather than dark horses. The model agrees: it gives them just a 6 percent chance of winning their group and 44 percent of surviving to the round of 32, with the odds thinning quickly after that — 14 percent to reach the last 16, under 4 percent to make a quarter-final, and a rounding-error 0.04 percent to lift the trophy. This is a side the numbers expect to be tested for survival, not asked to chase deep runs.

The recent form offers a more encouraging read than the rankings. Across their last ten they lost only to higher-grade opposition — Netherlands, Canada and Uruguay — while keeping clean sheets in goalless draws with Iran and Venezuela and beating Egypt 2-0. The defence looks the backbone here, with Khusanov, Ashurmatov and a deep goalkeeping pool, but the friendly defeats to Canada and the Netherlands hint at the gap to the level they will meet.

Jordan.text:
Jordan arrive ranked 63rd in the world with one of the lighter squads in the field at €20.3m, and the model treats them accordingly: a 14.3% chance of reaching the round of 32, just 1.6% to make the round of 16, and a 0.5% shot at topping their group. Champion odds round to zero. That is the cold arithmetic for Al Nashama, and the recent friendlies do little to argue against it — a 0-2 loss to Colombia and a 1-4 defeat in Switzerland were sandwiched by 2-2 draws with Nigeria and Costa Rica.

The encouraging note sits a little further back. At December's Arab Cup, Jordan strung together five straight wins, beating Egypt 3-0 away and edging both Iraq and Saudi Arabia 1-0, before Morocco caught them 3-2. That run shows a side capable of stifling decent opposition and taking its chances when it counts. The model still sees a steep climb here, but the difference between Jordan's Arab Cup form and their spring results is the question their group stage will answer.

Qatar.text:
Qatar arrive at the World Cup with their form pointing the wrong way. The Maroons, ranked 55th, have won just once in their last ten outings — a 2-1 home win over the United Arab Emirates in qualifying — while shipping heavy defeats to Tunisia (0-3) and Russia (1-4) and going scoreless in five of those matches, including the most recent goalless friendly draw with El Salvador. With a squad valued at just €19.93m, the attacking threat looks thin, and the run through the December Arab Cup, where they lost to both Palestine and Tunisia, did little to suggest momentum is building.

The model reflects that. It gives Qatar only an 11.3% chance — barely one in nine — of even clearing the round of 32, and a 23.56% probability of going out at that first knockout hurdle. Beyond that the path narrows sharply: a 2.85% shot at the round of 16, 0.24% at the quarter-finals, and effectively no chance of lifting the trophy. On current evidence, simply being competitive in the group stage would represent a respectable showing for this side.

New Zealand.text:
New Zealand arrive as Oceania's representatives ranked 85th in the world, and the recent form makes for grim reading: one win in their last ten, a 4-1 dismantling of Chile in March the lone bright spot against a run that includes 0-4 to Haiti, 0-1 to England, and back-to-back defeats to Australia. With a squad valued at just €34.35m and a goal threat that often dries up entirely — five clean sheets conceded among those ten games where they failed to score — this is a side built more in hope than expectation.

The model sees it much the same way. It gives the All Whites only a 6.4% chance of winning their group and a 40.8% shot at surviving into the round of 32, which in the expanded format is close to a coin flip you would not want to take. Beyond that the door shuts quickly: 10.7% to reach the last 16, 1.9% to make the quarter-finals, and a vanishing 0.01% to lift the trophy. The honest read is that simply getting out of the group would already exceed what the numbers expect.

Norway.text:
Norway arrive ranked 31st in the world but carrying a squad valued at €589.9m and the kind of qualifying form that turns heads: a 4-1 win away to Italy in November, an 11-1 demolition of Moldova, and a 5-0 of Israel before that. The friendly results since have been flatter — a 1-1 draw in Morocco, a 0-0 with Switzerland and a 1-2 loss in the Netherlands either side of a 3-1 win over Sweden — so the picture is of a side that can overwhelm weaker opponents but hasn't yet proven it can grind out results against peers.

The model reads that ambivalence into the numbers. Norway are 81.3% to clear the group into the round of 32 and 43.4% to reach the last 16, but only 20.5% to actually win their group, and the deeper rounds thin out fast: 18.8% to the quarters, 7.6% to a semi-final, and just 0.98% to lift the trophy. This is a team the model rates as a likely knockout participant rather than a contender — capable of a run, but a long shot to go all the way.

Sweden.text:
Sweden arrive ranked 38th and carrying a squad worth €406m, but the recent form makes that look generous. The autumn was grim: back-to-back defeats to Kosovo, two losses to Switzerland including a 4-1 hammering, and only draws against Slovenia left them scrapping. Things turned in March with a 3-1 win away to Ukraine and a 3-2 home win over Poland, before the summer friendlies cooled the mood again — a 1-3 loss to Norway and a 2-2 draw with Greece. A defence built around Lindelöf, Hien and Lagerbielke has the names, but it has been leaking goals when it matters.

The model reflects all that. It gives Sweden barely a coin-flip to clear the opening stage at 50.0%, only 12.8% to reach the round of 16, and just 8.4% to win their group outright. Beyond that the numbers thin out fast: 4.0% to the quarter-finals, under 1% to the semis, and 0.03% to lift the trophy. This is a team the model treats as a likely early exit, and the qualifying evidence is hard to argue with.

Czech Republic.text:
Recognised by FIFA as Czechia, the Czech Republic arrive ranked 41st in the world and without the security blanket of home advantage. Their qualifying campaign was a study in not losing rather than dominating: 2-2 with the Republic of Ireland, 1-1 with Denmark, a 0-0 against Croatia and a 2-0 win in Montenegro all came alongside a 6-0 thrashing of Gibraltar. The blot was a 1-2 defeat away to the Faroe Islands in October, a result that still stings against the run of an otherwise unbeaten recent stretch that includes friendly wins over Kosovo and Guatemala in the build-up.

The model treats them as a side likely to survive the opening round but no further. It gives them a 60.8% chance of reaching the round of 32 and just an 11.7% shot at winning their group, with the knockout odds thinning fast: 19.3% to make the last 16, 4.2% to reach the quarters and only 0.03% to lift the trophy. With a squad valued at €188m and built on a deep goalkeeping pool, this is a team measured by its floor rather than its ceiling.

Bosnia and Herzegovina.text:
Bosnia and Herzegovina arrive ranked 65th in the world and valued at a modest €151.6m, and the model treats them accordingly: a near coin-flip 40.4% to survive the group stage into the round of 32, but only 7% to reach the round of 16 and 2.5% to win their group. Beyond that the path thins quickly, with a quarter-final priced at under 1% and the latter rounds effectively off the table. This is a side the numbers expect to be competitive without ever threatening the favourites.

The form arrives mixed but rarely heavy. Their last ten show plenty of draws — 1-1 results against Wales and Italy in qualifying, a goalless friendly with FYR Macedonia and a 1-1 in Panama — alongside the bigger nights, a 3-1 over Romania and a 6-0 thrashing of San Marino. The lone defeat was a 1-2 home loss to Austria. A squad built on depth in goal, with Jurkas, Vasilj and Zlomislic, and an experienced defensive core including Kolasinac and Katic, looks organised enough to make their group games tight, which is broadly what the 40% survival figure implies.

Algeria.text:
Algeria arrive ranked 28th in the world and carrying a squad valued at €256.9m, and their recent form backs up the billing. The friendly win away to the Netherlands on 3 June, a goalless draw with Uruguay and a 7-0 demolition of Guatemala suggest a side in good rhythm, while their Africa Cup of Nations run produced wins over Congo DR, Equatorial Guinea, Burkina Faso and Sudan before a 2-0 semi-final loss to Nigeria. With Aït-Nouri and Bensebaïni anchoring the defence, this is a team that keeps clean sheets — five in the last ten.

The model sees a side likely to advance but unlikely to go deep. It gives Algeria an 84.6% chance of surviving the group stage into the round of 32, but only 30.4% to reach the last 16 and 15% to actually win their group. From there the odds thin quickly: 6.2% to reach a semi-final, 2.3% to make the final and just 0.7% to lift the trophy. In short, expect Algeria to be there in the knockouts, but the numbers frame a quarter-final as their realistic ceiling.

Ghana.text:
Ghana arrive ranked 74th in the world, and the model treats them as long shots: a 3.77% chance of winning their group and just a 9.58% shot at reaching the last 16, with the simulations effectively writing off any deeper run at 0.01% to lift the trophy. The squad is valued at €234.6m, but the recent evidence is uncomfortable. Qualifying went smoothly — four unbeaten across the autumn, including a 5-0 thumping of the Central African Republic and narrow home wins over Mali and Comoros — yet the friendlies that followed told a harsher story.

Against tougher opposition the Black Stars have looked exposed, shipping five to Austria and losing to Germany, Japan, South Korea and Mexico before salvaging a 1-1 draw with Wales in June. That gap between qualifying comfort and friendly struggles is the central question here: the model's near coin-flip survival number (47.36% to clear the group stage into the round of 32) leans on the wins, while the defeats hint the floor could be lower. With the squad's goal-scoring contributions unlisted, the attacking burden is hard to pin down, which only adds to the uncertainty.

Cape Verde.text:
Cape Verde arrive at their first World Cup off the back of the qualifying run that got them here, and the recent form reads well: the Tubarões Azuis beat Cameroon 1-0 and Eswatini 3-0 in African qualifying, then warmed up with 3-0 home wins over Serbia and Bermuda in June. That's four clean-sheet victories in their last campaign-to-tournament stretch, the sort of momentum a debutant wants. The caveats are real too — a 4-2 loss in Chile and goalless or low-scoring draws away to Iran and Egypt show the gap to sterner opposition.

Ranked 69th in the world with a squad valued at €54.5m, Cape Verde are among the lighter sides in the field, and the model treats them accordingly. It gives them a 31% chance of surviving the group into the round of 32, but only 4.3% to reach the last 16 and 1.4% to actually win their group. Beyond that the numbers thin out fast — under 1% to reach the quarter-finals. Realistically this is a tournament about competing hard and seeing whether that defensive solidity travels.

Curaçao.text:
Curaçao arrive at their first World Cup as the smallest nation by population ever to qualify, ranked 82nd in the world with a squad valued at just €25.78m. Their route through CONCACAF qualifying was the story: unbeaten across the run shown here, with home wins over Jamaica (2-0) and goalless draws away to Trinidad and Tobago and Jamaica that did the quiet work. The 7-0 demolition of Bermuda showed the ceiling, and a back line built around Bazoer, Brenet and Floranus gives them a recognisable spine.

The warm-up friendlies are the sobering counterweight. Defeats of 1-4 to Scotland, 1-5 to Australia and 0-2 to China against tougher opposition hint at how far the gap stretches once they leave their confederation, even if a 4-0 win over Aruba steadied the mood. The model treats this realistically: just an 8.1% chance of reaching the round of 32 and 0.5% of winning the group, with the knockout numbers thinning to almost nothing beyond. Qualifying was the achievement here; everything in the tournament itself would be genuine overperformance against the odds.

Haiti.text:
Haiti reached the 2026 World Cup the hard way, grinding through CONCACAF qualification with home wins over Costa Rica (1-0) and Nicaragua (2-0) to close out the campaign, after earlier holding Costa Rica to a 3-3 draw away and stifling Honduras to a goalless stalemate at home. The blot was a 0-3 loss in Honduras, a reminder of the ceiling when the defence is stretched. Ranked 83rd in the world with a squad valued at €55.9m, this is a side built on goalkeeping depth and a back line stocked with names like Duverne, Delcroix and Ade rather than marquee attacking talent.

Warm-up form has been mixed: a 4-0 thumping of New Zealand alongside a 1-2 defeat to Peru and a narrow loss to Tunisia. The model sees a tough draw ahead, giving Haiti just a 24.6% chance of surviving the group into the round of 32 and only 4.7% of reaching the last 16. Winning the group sits at 1.4%, and anything beyond the quarter-finals — pegged at 0.9% — looks fanciful. Survival, not a deep run, is the realistic measure of success here.

Iraq.text:
Iraq reached the 2026 finals the hard way, edging Bolivia 2-1 in April's intercontinental play-off after a qualifying campaign that included a goalless draw away to Saudi Arabia and a home win over the United Arab Emirates. The recent form reads steadily rather than spectacularly: a creditable 1-1 draw away to Spain in June, a narrow win over Andorra, and a mixed Arab Cup that brought wins against Sudan and Bahrain but defeats to Algeria and Jordan. Ranked 57th and carrying a squad valued at just €21.2m — among the lighter ones in the field — the Lions of Mesopotamia arrive as clear underdogs.

The model sees them that way too. It gives Iraq a 13.5% chance of escaping the group into the round of 32, but only 2.3% to reach the round of 16 and a 0.4% shot at the quarter-finals, with the title priced at effectively zero. Winning the group is a 0.9% long shot. That Spain result hints Iraq can frustrate stronger sides for spells; the numbers suggest doing so over a full tournament is another matter entirely.

H. Kane.text:
Kane's club output isn't tailing off at 32 — it's accelerating. His 2024 at Bayern was already elite, 26 Bundesliga goals in 31 games plus 11 in 13 Champions League outings. The 2025 campaign topped it: 36 league goals in the same 31 appearances, 14 in 13 in Europe, and a remarkable 10 in six DFB Pokal ties, with ratings climbing to 8.0 in the Champions League and 8.05 in the cup. That is a striker improving his finishing volume rather than coasting on reputation.

It carries straight into England's colours. Eight goals in eight World Cup qualifiers across 2024, then two in two and a 8.1 rating to open the 2026 cycle, suggest the supply line and the form are intact. For a side ranked fourth in the world, that is the profile you want leading the line: a forward scoring at better than a goal a game for his club and converting his international chances just as ruthlessly. The model has every reason to lean on him.

J. David.text:
Jonathan David arrives at a World Cup on his home continent with a club CV that pulls in two directions. The 2024 version was a genuine elite forward: 16 goals in 32 Ligue 1 appearances for Lille at a 7.15 rating, with another nine in Champions League minutes. The 2025 move to Juventus dulled that edge — six Serie A goals across 35 appearances and a rating that slipped to 6.43, with his nine Champions League outings rated a modest 6.30. That's a striker producing less and rated lower in a tougher tactical setting, which tempers expectations of him carrying Canada's attack on volume alone.

The encouraging counterweight is that he keeps scoring in a Canada shirt. Even through the leaner club spell he managed three friendly goals across 2025, and his 2026 sample, though tiny at 169 minutes, shows two goals and a 7.23 rating. At 25 he remains Canada's likeliest finisher, just not the runaway one his Lille numbers once implied.

Mikel Oyarzabal.text: Oyarzabal's club output is trending the right way at exactly the right time. His La Liga scoring jumped from nine goals in 37 appearances across 2024 to 15 in 34 in 2025, a step up in efficiency for a 28-year-old hitting his peak. The cup runs reinforce it: an 8.28 rating across four Copa del Rey ties in 2024, then three goals in six 2025 outings. What matters most for Spain is that this form has transferred to the national team. He scored six goals with four assists in just six World Cup qualifiers, a 7.88 rating, and added efficient returns from limited minutes in Nations League and friendly fixtures. The 2026 friendlies, two goals in only 63 minutes at an 8.7 rating, fit the pattern of a forward who needs little time to influence a game. For the second-ranked side in the world, a finisher this productive in qualifying is a genuine asset, even if the friendly sample is small enough to treat the rating with caution.

Kylian Mbappé.text:
Two seasons at Real Madrid have settled any question about whether Mbappé travels: 31 La Liga goals in 34 games across 2024, then 25 in 31 the following campaign, the kind of output that doesn't dip. The most striking line is his Champions League return in 2025 — 15 goals in just 11 appearances at an 8.05 rating, suggesting he raises his level as the opposition does, exactly the trait that matters in a knockout tournament. International form points the same way: five goals and three assists in only four World Cup qualifiers, an 8.38 rating that tops everything else here.

The one caution sits in the most recent data. Across four France friendlies in 2026 he has a single goal in 219 minutes at a 7.03 rating, a quieter run than his club numbers. With France ranked first in the world, that reads more like rotation and friendly intensity than decline, but it's the one strand worth watching as the tournament nears rather than assuming the Madrid scoring rate carries over untouched.

Lautaro Martínez.text:
The dossier here tracks a goalkeeper — Aston Villa's number one, listed in full as Damián Emiliano Martínez Romero — so the story is about minutes and ratings rather than goals. The trend worth flagging is a quiet downward drift. In 2024 he posted a 7.09 rating across 37 Premier League games, peaking at 7.42 over 1,080 Champions League minutes and 7.33 at the Copa América. By 2025 the league number had slipped to 6.98 over 32 appearances, and across 16 World Cup qualifiers in the current cycle he sits at 6.93 from 1,459 minutes.

That points to a still-reliable but no longer ascending keeper heading into the tournament at 33, behind a third-ranked Argentina side. None of these competitions show a single goal conceded broken out, so we're reading form off ratings alone — solid, consistent, but cooling rather than peaking. For a knockout run that leans on its goalkeeper, that gentle decline is the number to keep an eye on rather than panic over.

E. Haaland.text:
Haaland's club output reads like a metronome: 22 Premier League goals in 32 appearances across 2024, then 27 in 35 across 2025, the latter sharpened by eight assists rather than just finishing. The Champions League tells the same story twice — eight goals in each campaign from roughly 760 minutes — so the elite-defence test does nothing to slow him. That consistency at the highest club level is the strongest argument for treating him as a tournament-defining centre-forward.

The international ledger is even more emphatic when the chances come: 16 goals in eight World Cup qualifiers and seven in six Nations League games during 2024, a strike rate Norway lives off. The caution is recency — two goals in two 2026 qualifiers but a blank in two friendlies (6.5 rating). Norway sit 31st in FIFA's ranking, so service, not the finisher, is the variable. Give Haaland the volume of chances his record demands and the goals follow; starve him and the supporting cast has to deliver.

Cristiano Ronaldo.text:
At 40, Ronaldo's club output hasn't dipped at all — if anything it's climbing. His Al-Nassr Pro League haul went from 25 goals in 30 games across 2024 to 28 in 30 across 2025, the kind of penalty-box scoring rate that travels regardless of league quality. The 7.16 league rating in 2025 sits a touch below 2024's 7.53, hinting his all-round game is narrowing toward pure finishing, but the goals keep coming.

For Portugal that profile is double-edged. He buried eight in eight Nations League outings and five in five World Cup qualifiers, so the international tap is still on. Yet his most recent appearance, a 45-minute friendly cameo rated 6.5, is a reminder that minutes management is now part of the picture. Expect Portugal, ranked fifth in the world, to feed him chances and lean on his record, while accepting he may be a sharp half-game weapon rather than a 90-minute fixture this summer.

R. Jiménez.text:
At 34, Raúl Jiménez is leaning on the gentle downward slope of his Fulham output rather than falling off it. His 2024 was the high-water mark: 12 Premier League goals from 38 games at a 7.01 rating, the kind of reliable centre-forward season that anchors a side. The 2025 campaign cooled to nine goals in 36 league matches and a 6.68 rating, still useful production but a clear step down in both finishing and overall influence. That drift matters for a tournament summer, because a year-on-year decline at his age rarely reverses on its own.

What keeps him interesting is the Mexico shirt. The same player who looked ordinary in club friendlies in 2025 (6.77) produced five goals in three Nations League games in 2024 at an 8.77 rating. The international version of Jiménez has historically outscored the club version, so treat his Fulham numbers as a floor rather than a ceiling — but expect a supporting scorer now, not a talisman.

L. Messi.text:
At 38, Messi is not winding down on the numbers we have. His MLS output has climbed every season: 21 goals and 11 assists in 22 games across 2024, a remarkable 35 and 23 in 31 games through 2025, and 13 goals with seven assists in 14 matches so far in 2026, his club rating ticking up from 8.33 to 8.67. The reinvention is worth noting too — Inter Miami now list him as a midfielder rather than an out-and-out attacker, which suggests a deeper, creative role that asks less of his legs.

For Argentina the signal is just as strong. He scored eight and assisted three in 12 World Cup qualifiers at an 8.03 rating, so the production travels to the international stage. The one caveat is workload: his 2026 national-team friendly minutes are minimal, just 35 across three caps, hinting at managed fitness. On this evidence he arrives as a central creator for a third-ranked side, with form that belies his age.

Brahim Díaz.text:
A Real Madrid career in miniature: useful but rarely central. His 2024 La Liga return of four goals and two assists across 31 appearances and just 1,405 minutes told the story of a rotation forward, and 2025 sharpened it — one goal but six assists in 30 league games, with his rating drifting from 6.86 to 6.68 as his minutes shrank. The Champions League work mirrors that, a goal apiece across both seasons off limited time.

What changes everything is the shirt. For Morocco, Díaz is a different player: seven goals in four Africa Cup of Nations qualifiers at an 8.22 rating, then five more across seven finals matches, where he was deployed as an attacker rather than a squad midfielder. That's the version, freed and central, that matters here. The caveat is recent: just one goal in four friendlies through 2026 at a modest 6.88. So the ceiling is real and proven at international level, but the form line into the tournament is quieter than the headline numbers suggest.

M. Olise.text:
Olise has turned two Bayern seasons into one continuous upward line. His first, in 2024, already read like a star's: 12 goals and 15 assists in 34 Bundesliga games at a 7.92 rating, with five more goals in the Champions League. The 2025 campaign was better still — 15 goals and 19 assists across 32 league outings, a 27-goal-contribution haul at a near-identical 7.89, plus five goals and six assists in Europe. That is a 24-year-old getting more productive, not coasting.

The France numbers point the same way. He posted an 8.42 rating across five World Cup qualifiers with two goals, and has opened 2026 with three goals and an assist in four friendlies. For the top-ranked team in the field, that is a creator and finisher arriving in form rather than hoping to find it. Minutes won't be the question; whether France lean on him as a primary threat is the only real one.

F. Wirtz.text:
Wirtz arrives at the tournament with a career still finding its level after a move. His 2024 was the high-water mark: 10 goals and 12 assists in the Bundesliga at a 7.7 rating, plus six goals in nine Champions League outings. The first Liverpool season cooled that down — five goals and three assists across 33 Premier League games at 6.81, his lowest sustained rating in the dossier, and just one goal in 11 Champions League appearances. That dip is the honest caveat on his form heading in.

The more recent signal is brighter, and it comes in a Germany shirt. Across the 2026 friendlies he has three goals and two assists in four games at a 7.95 rating, echoing the 7.83 and 7.97 he posted in Nations League and World Cup qualifying. At 22 he reads as a player whose club output stalled but whose international ceiling stays high — a creator Germany lean on, even if Polymarket should treat the recent club season as a reason for caution.

B. Embolo.text:
Embolo's club output has trended the right way at the most relevant moment. His 2024 was a steady Monaco season — six goals and four assists across 29 Ligue 1 starts, plus a creative Champions League cameo of one goal and three assists, but a modest 6.75 rating. The 2025 switch to Rennes sharpened the goalscoring: nine Ligue 1 goals in 31 games, his best return in the dossier, even if the 6.71 rating suggests the all-round contribution stayed level rather than spiking.

What lifts the tournament read is his Switzerland form, which consistently outpaces his club numbers. He scored four in six World Cup qualifiers (7.23), three in four 2025 friendlies (7.05), and two more in three 2026 friendlies at a 7.27 rating. That pattern — a reliable rather than prolific club striker who reliably finds the net in national colours — makes him a credible focal point for a 19th-ranked Swiss side, and a sensible name in the anytime-scorer markets.

V. Gyökeres.text:
At Sporting in 2024, Gyökeres was a genuine phenomenon: 39 league goals in 33 games at a 7.99 rating, plus six more in eight Champions League outings. The 2025 move to Arsenal recalibrated those numbers sharply. Fourteen Premier League goals from 36 appearances is a respectable return, but the rating slid to 6.62 and the creative side dried up to a single league assist, with five goals in 13 Champions League games confirming a striker scoring steadily rather than dominating.

The encouraging thread for Sweden is that his international form has never tracked the club dip. He hit nine goals in six Nations League matches at an 8.65 rating, and kept scoring through 2025's friendlies with six in eight. For a side ranked 38th, that gap matters: Gyökeres is the one player capable of turning limited chances into goals at this level. Expect a focal-point centre-forward who needs service, but who finishes what he gets — exactly the profile a lower-ranked team leans on.

L. Díaz.text:
Díaz arrives at the World Cup in the best vein of his career. His last Liverpool season was solid without being spectacular — 13 goals in 36 Premier League games at a 7.27 rating — but the move to Bayern München lifted him to another level. Across 2025 he posted 15 goals and 14 assists in 32 Bundesliga matches, and was even sharper in Europe, with seven goals and three assists in 12 Champions League outings at a 7.52 rating, his best mark anywhere. That is a player no longer just a finisher but a genuine creator.

For Colombia the read is encouraging. He carried that form into qualifying, scoring seven and assisting three across 17 CONMEBOL games at a 7.25 rating, often operating in a deeper midfield role. At 28 he is squarely in his peak, and a side ranked 13th in the world will lean heavily on a forward producing at both ends of the pitch.

S. Mané.text:
At 33, Sadio Mané arrives in fine, if subtly shifting, form. His best recent return was the 2024 Pro League season at Al-Nassr — 14 goals and 11 assists across 32 games at a 7.56 rating, the numbers of a frontline match-winner. The 2025 campaign reads slightly cooler in front of goal, 10 goals and six assists from 25 league appearances, but the more telling detail is positional: he logged much of that season listed as a midfielder rather than an attacker, the rating holding steady at 7.28. That deeper role carried into the Africa Cup of Nations, where he played as a midfielder and produced two goals and three assists in seven games at 7.31.

For the tournament, that flexibility matters. Senegal, ranked 14th, can deploy him as a goal threat or a creator, and he is scoring either way — his lone 2026 friendly brought two goals and an 8.9 rating. The output is real; only the workload at his age is the open question.

Raphinha.text:
Raphinha arrives at this World Cup off the back of a genuinely elite club peak. His 2024 was the standout: 18 goals and nine assists in La Liga at a 7.8 rating, plus a remarkable 13 goals and eight assists in just 14 Champions League games, where his 8.25 rating was the best of any line on his card. The 2025 season saw a tactical shift, deployed more as a midfielder, and the raw output cooled slightly — 13 league goals in 22 appearances at 7.55, with the Champions League return dropping to three goals from seven games at 7.31.

For Brazil he has carried that into qualifying: five goals and two assists across 13 World Cup qualifiers at a 7.42 rating. The one flag is friendlies, where he has consistently dipped — 6.7 in 2024, 6.6 in 2026. The pattern suggests a player who delivers when the games matter, which is exactly what Brazil will need.

Vinícius Júnior.text:
Vinícius arrives off two strong club seasons but with a softer recent dip worth watching. His 2024 was the Champions League peak — eight goals in twelve appearances at a 7.56 rating, alongside 11 goals and eight assists in La Liga. The 2025 campaign pushed his scoring further, 16 league goals across 36 games at a 7.51 rating, though his Champions League returns leveled to five goals and five assists.

The flag is in the Brazil shirt. World Cup qualifying brought just two goals and one assist in 11 appearances, and his rating slipped to 6.93 — his lowest sustained run in the dossier, often deployed in midfield rather than as an out-and-out attacker. The pattern is clear: devastating in club colours, more muted for the national team. For a Brazil side ranked sixth, his ceiling is among the tournament's highest, but the qualifying numbers suggest the goals haven't reliably translated, and that gap is the open question heading into 2026.

K. De Bruyne.text:
At 34, De Bruyne's club output has cooled but not collapsed. His final Manchester City Premier League season returned four goals and seven assists across 33 games at a 7.32 rating, with a notably quieter Champions League return of 6.86 over seven appearances. The move to Napoli has kept him productive without restoring the old peak: five goals and two assists in 18 Serie A outings, a 7.04 rating, plus two assists in limited Champions League minutes.

The more encouraging signal is what he does for Belgium. He hit six goals in six World Cup qualifiers at an 8.27 rating in 2024, and posted 8.1 in the Nations League — numbers that dwarf his club ratings. His 2026 friendly form, a 7.33 across four games, sits comfortably above his Serie A baseline. For a ninth-ranked side, that gap matters: De Bruyne the international remains a tournament-level creator even as De Bruyne the week-in club player edges toward rotation.

Son Heung-Min.text:
Son's trajectory tells a clear story. His final Tottenham league season in 2024 was steady rather than spectacular — seven goals and nine assists across 30 appearances at a 7.26 rating. The move to Los Angeles FC reignited him: 12 goals in just 11 MLS games during 2025, an 8.18 rating that ranks as the standout number on his page. The 2026 club picture is murkier — zero league goals in 13 MLS outings, though eight assists and a 7.15 rating show he's still creating, just not finishing.

The encouraging signal for South Korea is that his international output hasn't dipped. Across World Cup qualifying he posted seven goals and four assists in 13 games at a 7.81 rating, his best competitive return anywhere recently. At 33, the club goal drought is worth watching, but a player who scores freely in a Korea shirt is exactly what they need. He arrives as the focal point of a side ranked 25th in the world, and on national-team form he's still delivering.

Bruno Fernandes.text:
At 31, Bruno Fernandes arrives in elite creative form rather than fading. His 2025 Premier League return is the standout line: nine goals and 21 assists across 35 appearances, a jump from the eight goals and ten assists he managed in 2024. That doubling of his assist output points to a player whose game is tilting from finisher to provider, which travels well to a tournament where Portugal will often dominate possession and need someone to unlock packed defences.

The 2024 Europa League run reinforces the case — seven goals in 14 games at an 8.05 rating, his best sustained number in the dossier. He has stayed durable too, logging over 3,000 league minutes in each of the last two seasons. The early 2026 sample is thin, a goal and an assist across three friendlies at a 7.5 rating, so we lean on the club body of work. For a side ranked fifth in the world, that profile makes him their most reliable source of chance creation.

S. Rahimi.text:
Rahimi's club graph points the wrong way heading into the tournament. In 2024 he was sharp for Al Ain, eight goals in 18 Pro League games at a 7.44 rating and a ruthless five in seven AFC Champions League Elite outings (7.54). The 2025 club return is thinner: two goals in 15 Pro League appearances, the rating down to 6.98, and a blank across three Club World Cup games at 6.85. His most recent club season, then, reads more like a steady starter than the matchwinner of a year earlier.

The caveat is that international Rahimi has kept scoring while club Rahimi cooled — five goals in three Africa Cup of Nations games and three in five AFCON qualifiers. For an eighth-ranked Morocco, that split is the open question: the dossier shows a player who lifts for his country even as the league numbers fade, so treat his finishing as live without leaning on the recent club form to project it.

C. Gakpo.text:
Gakpo's club trajectory tells a two-season story worth weighing. The 2024 campaign was his peak at Liverpool: 10 goals and four assists in 35 Premier League games at a 7.22 rating, with a ruthless League Cup return of five goals in six. The 2025 season cooled, with the output dipping to seven goals and five assists across 36 league outings and a 6.89 rating as he was redeployed deeper into midfield, his Champions League contribution thinning to two goals in 11.

That domestic flattening matters, but it isn't the whole picture. In a Netherlands shirt he has consistently produced more than for his club, topped by four goals and four assists in World Cup qualifying at an 8.06 rating. The early 2026 friendlies extend that, two goals in four games at 7.85. For a seventh-ranked side, the read is a forward whose club numbers have softened yet who keeps scoring for his country — a reason to expect end product without banking on his Liverpool peak.

M. Taremi.text: Taremi's story is one of a player rediscovered. His final season at Inter was a peripheral one — just 816 Serie A minutes across 26 appearances for a single goal at a 6.7 rating, though his Champions League work held up better with a 7.08 rating and three assists. The summer switch to Olympiakos restored him to a central role, and the numbers followed: 10 goals in 24 Super League games at a 7.07 rating, plus two more in the Champions League. That regularity has carried into national-team duty, where his World Cup qualifying campaign for 21st-ranked Iran returned five goals and four assists from 15 outings at a standout 7.76 rating. The implication for the tournament is encouraging. At 33 he is no longer a Serie A bit-part figure but a first-choice forward in rhythm, and his qualifying output suggests Iran's attack runs through him. Minutes and sharpness, the two things missing in Milan, are no longer in question.

A. Ueda.text:
Ueda's scoring curve points one direction: up. The 2024 Eredivisie return of seven goals in 21 appearances was useful rather than prolific, but the 2025 league campaign reshaped his reputation entirely — 25 goals in 31 games at a 7.17 rating, the work of a genuine penalty-box finisher hitting his peak years at 27. That domestic surge has carried straight into the national team, where his nine World Cup qualifiers brought eight goals and two assists at a tournament-best 7.49 rating. For a Japan side ranked 18th in the world, that is a centre-forward arriving in form rather than hoping to find it.

The honest caveat sits in his most recent friendlies: three caps, 138 minutes, no goals and a 6.37 rating. It's a small sample against the weight of his Eredivisie and qualifying output, but it's a reminder that his European elite-competition numbers stayed modest. The model leans on the league and qualifying evidence, and that evidence is loud.

Lamine Yamal.text: At 18, Lamine Yamal's club trajectory points firmly upward. His 2024 La Liga return was already strong for a teenager — 9 goals and 13 assists across 34 appearances at a 7.79 rating — but the 2025 season is where the output sharpened into something more dangerous. In fewer minutes and fewer games (28 apps), he nearly doubled his goals to 16 while still adding 11 assists, lifting his rating to 7.95. The Champions League tells the same story: 5 goals and 3 assists in 2024 became 6 goals and 4 assists in 2025, with his rating climbing to a tournament-best 8.14. The shift toward a midfield role hasn't dulled the numbers — it's broadened them. The one note of caution is 2026 itself: two Spain friendlies, 108 minutes, no goal involvements and a 7.1 rating. A tiny, low-stakes sample, but worth watching for whether his blistering club form carries directly into the summer with the world's second-ranked side.

Pedri.text:
Pedri arrives at 23 as the metronome of a Barcelona side he's anchored across two heavy seasons, and the numbers point to a player shifting from scorer to creator. His 2024 La Liga return was a tidy 7.62 rating with four goals and five assists across 2,808 minutes; in 2025 the goals dried up to two but the assists jumped to nine in 2,107 league minutes, a 7.54 rating that reflects deeper involvement rather than decline. The Champions League is where the dip shows, ratings sliding to 7.19, so the very top level remains the question mark.

For the tournament, the most telling line is his Spain output: a standout 8.5 rating with two goals in four World Cup qualifiers, comfortably his best work in the dataset. That international lift, behind a side ranked second in the world, matters more than three flat 2026 friendly appearances (7.1, no goals or assists). The body of work says steady, high-floor midfield control rather than a goal threat.

Vitinha.text:
Vitinha has built two highly consistent seasons at the heart of Paris Saint-Germain, and the ratings tell the story before the goals do. In 2024 he posted a 7.52 in Ligue 1 and a near-identical 7.56 across 17 Champions League appearances, chipping in five league goals. The 2025 campaign saw the output shift rather than fade: just one league goal but seven assists at a 7.58 rating, while his Champions League contribution jumped to six goals from 17 matches at the same 7.58 mark. That is a midfielder trusted with heavy minutes — over 2,000 in Ligue 1 each year — and delivering at elite level in both phases of play.

For a Portugal side ranked fifth in the world, that reliability matters. He rarely dips below a 7.2 in any competition, and his three Nations League assists hint at the creative role he can fill. The form points to a player arriving at the tournament settled, durable and in genuinely top condition.

Ousmane Dembélé.text:
Dembélé arrives as the attacking spearhead of the world's top-ranked side, and his club numbers explain why. The 2024 campaign was the peak: 21 goals and seven assists in 30 Ligue 1 games at a 7.72 rating, plus eight goals and six assists across 15 Champions League outings. The 2025 follow-up cooled slightly without falling off — 10 league goals in 22 starts and another eight in Europe, though his minutes nearly halved, hinting at a more rotated role.

The caveat is France itself. For all the PSG output, his international scoring has been strangely dry: zero goals across five Euro appearances and two friendlies this cycle, often used in midfield rather than as a pure attacker. That positional shuffle is the thing to watch. A player producing a goal involvement roughly every other club match should be a genuine tournament threat, but only if France play him where he scores rather than where he tracks back.

Rayan Cherki.text:
At 22, Cherki has handled a step up without losing his playmaking edge. His 2024 at Lyon was a creative high-water mark: 8 goals and 11 assists across 30 Ligue 1 games at a 7.63 rating, with the Europa League run even sharper, four goals and eight assists at an 8.02 mark over 12 appearances. The 2025 move to Manchester City reframed him as more provider than scorer — 12 assists but only four goals in 33 Premier League outings, the rating settling to a still-strong 7.20, with three goals apiece in the Champions League and League Cup to show he can finish when the chances fall.

For the tournament, that profile reads as a high-assist, secondary-scoring threat from midfield who has now tested himself at the top European level. France, ranked first in the world, can use him as a creator off the bench or in rotation; his three France friendlies in 2026 (one goal, 7.27) suggest he is already inside the picture.

Jude Bellingham.text:
Bellingham's club output has cooled rather than collapsed. His first full Real Madrid season in 2024 set a high bar: 9 goals and 8 assists across 31 La Liga games at a 7.48 rating, backed by 3 goals and 3 assists in the Champions League. The 2025 campaign reads a step down — 6 goals and 4 assists in 28 league appearances, with the rating slipping to 7.28 and his Champions League mark easing to 7.2. That's still strong production from midfield, but the goal involvement per minute has clearly thinned.

The more encouraging signal for the tournament comes in an England shirt. He posted an 8.33 rating across four Nations League outings when pushed into a more advanced attacking role in 2024, and his early 2026 World Cup qualifying form is bright at 7.6 with an assist. England rank fourth in the world, and a Bellingham deployed higher up the pitch has historically been their most dangerous version of him.

Unai Simón.text:
The numbers paint Unai Simón as a dependable rather than spectacular presence, and a heavily used one. His club workload jumped from 25 La Liga appearances in 2024 to a full 37 across 3,330 minutes in 2025, a near-ever-present season that nudged his domestic rating down only marginally, from 7.09 to 6.88. The European step-up tells a similar story: he held a 7.22 across six Euro 2024 games for Spain but managed a more modest 6.5 over eight Champions League outings with Athletic last term, with the odd low — a 4.6 in the 2025 Super Cup — dragging at the edges.
