For a 28-year-old keeping goal for the world's second-ranked side, that's the profile of a steady operator who has shown he can sustain a punishing schedule. His two 2026 friendlies for Spain returned a 7.3, a small but encouraging sign heading in. Expect competence and durability rather than highlight-reel saves swinging Spain's tournament.

J. Pickford.text: Pickford arrives at 31 as the settled custodian behind a side ranked fourth by FIFA, and his club record reads like a metronome: an ever-present 38 Premier League appearances in both 2024 and 2025, 3,420 then 3,432 minutes, with no rotation to speak of. The durability matters for a tournament where a first-choice keeper can play seven games inside a month. What gives slight pause is the trend in the numbers. His Everton league rating eased from 7.26 in 2024 to 7.02 in 2025, and his most recent international work has dipped further — 6.90 across two World Cup qualifiers and 6.75 over two friendlies in 2026. That is a soft decline rather than a collapse, and small samples in qualifying carry their own noise, but it nudges his level back toward the 6.8-7.0 band rather than the 7.2-plus peaks of his cup outings. For a deep England run, steady is the realistic expectation, not spectacular.

Y. Bounou.text:
Yassine Bounou heads to the tournament as a settled, in-form number one. His Al-Hilal league work has been steady rather than spectacular — 6.82 across 31 Pro League games in 2024, nudging up to 6.93 over 26 appearances in 2025 — but the pattern that matters here is how he raises his level on the big stage. He posted 7.42 in the AFC Champions League Elite in 2024 and a striking 7.78 across five FIFA Club World Cup outings in 2025, exactly the high-stakes, knockout environment a World Cup demands.

The Morocco numbers reinforce it: 7.17 over seven games at the Africa Cup of Nations and 7.33 in three 2026 friendlies. At 34 he is not declining, and a goalkeeper who consistently lifts his rating when the games get bigger is a quiet asset for a side ranked eighth in the world. For a team that may need to win matches by margins, that kind of reliability behind them is worth plenty.

M. Maignan.text:
At 30, Mike Maignan brings a goalkeeper's most valuable asset to France's number one ranking: consistency. His Serie A return is almost metronomic — 37 appearances at a 7.1 average in the 2024 campaign, then 37 again at a slightly improved 7.23 in 2025, more than 3,300 minutes each season with no sign of rotation. The Champions League sample backs it up, ten outings at 7.22, and his cup work has touched 7.7 in the Coppa Italia. That is the profile of a settled, near ever-present starter heading into the tournament, exactly what a side topping the FIFA rankings wants behind it.

The caution sits with the national team. His France friendly ratings have drifted to 6.9, then 6.83 in the early 2026 window, and his World Cup qualifying mark of 6.62 is the lowest number on his sheet. Club form says elite; the recent international readings are merely solid, and that gap is worth watching once the games carry weight.

Alisson Becker.text:
Alisson's club numbers tell a story of gentle decline from a very high base. His 2024 was his strongest run, a 7.11 average across 28 Premier League games and a standout 7.62 over six Champions League nights. The 2025 league campaign softened that picture: 26 appearances at 6.78, his weakest sustained rating in the dossier, even as his European form held firmer at 7.18. The encouraging signal for Brazil is that he has rediscovered something on international duty, posting 7.18 across nine World Cup qualifiers — comfortably above his recent Liverpool league baseline and right back at his 2024 Copa America level.

For a 33-year-old goalkeeper, that mix of a slightly dipping club floor and a solid qualifying ceiling reads as steady rather than commanding. Brazil sit sixth in the FIFA ranking, and Alisson remains a dependable rather than spectacular last line — enough to keep his side competitive, short of the form that single-handedly wins knockout ties.

Diogo Costa.text:
Costa is Porto's undisputed number one and the trend is gently upward. Across 2024 he logged 32 Primeira Liga starts at a 6.98 rating, with a slightly softer 6.89 in the Europa League. The 2025 season tightened that picture: 33 league appearances and 2,907 minutes at 7.07, plus 7.05 in Europe and a tidy 7.33 across his Taça de Portugal outings. There's no drama in these numbers, which for a goalkeeper is exactly the point — he plays nearly every minute and his form holds steady rather than spiking or sagging.

The international sample flatters him further. He posted 7.34 at the Euros and 7.23 in the Nations League, both comfortably above his club baseline, even if a 6.60 across six World Cup qualifiers is a reminder that the floor exists. For a team ranked fifth in the world, that combination — durable, rarely below 7, and demonstrably capable of raising his level on the biggest stage — is close to ideal heading into the tournament.

T. Courtois.text:
At 33, Thibaut Courtois remains Real Madrid's first choice and the numbers say his level has held remarkably steady. Across the 2024 season he averaged 7.11 over 32 La Liga starts and lifted that to 7.43 in the Champions League, the bigger stage drawing the better performances. The 2025 campaign reads similarly: another 32 La Liga appearances at 6.93, but 7.29 in Europe and a standout 7.47 across six Club World Cup outings, the same pattern of rising to elite opposition. That is exactly the profile a tournament side wants behind it, and Belgium, ranked ninth by FIFA, will lean on it heavily.

His early 2026 form offers a small but encouraging marker, a 7.05 average across two friendlies as he settles back into the national setup. There is no decline visible here; if anything his knockout-football ratings are his best work. For a Belgium team whose ceiling depends on tight games staying tight, that is a genuine asset.

T. Hayakawa.text:
Tomoki Hayakawa has been a model of consistency in goal for Kashima, and the trend is the encouraging part. He played every minute he could in the J1 League across 2024 and 2025 — 38 appearances and 3,420 minutes in each — and his rating climbed from 7.06 to 7.15. The 2026 season has him rising again: 7.38 across 17 league outings, comfortably his best return and the kind of form that earns a goalkeeper a hard look ahead of a tournament.

The caveat is at international level, where the picture is thinner. His Japan minutes have come mostly in the EAFF E-1 Championship and friendlies, and his rating there (6.64 in the larger sample) sits below his club number, including an eight-minute friendly cameo in 2026. At 26 and 187cm, the profile is solid and improving, but his ceiling for the World Cup likely depends on whether that club form translates against stiffer opposition than he has faced for the national side so far.

O. Baumann.text:
Baumann arrives at 35 with a heavy recent workload but a quietly slipping line on the chart. His 2024 was his steadiest in a Hoffenheim shirt, a 7.01 Bundesliga rating across 28 games, backed by solid international cameos — 7.00 in the Nations League and 6.92 over six World Cup qualifiers. The 2025 league season was busier still, a full 34 appearances and 3,060 minutes, but the rating eased to 6.81, the marks of a goalkeeper playing a lot rather than playing better.

The flag for the tournament is the 2026 sample: three friendlies, 270 minutes, a 6.27 average that sits well below anything in his club record. It is a small sample and friendlies are friendlies, but for a 35-year-old keeper behind a Germany side ranked tenth, the trend points down rather than up. The dossier shows a dependable, high-volume career number drifting the wrong way at exactly the wrong moment, which is reason for measured caution rather than confidence.

preview.text:
Home advantage doesn't get much more emphatic than the Azteca, and the numbers frame this as a clear mismatch. Mexico arrive 15th in the world and worth €191.85m to South Africa's €49.25m, riding a run capped by a 5-1 friendly win over Serbia, while Bafana have stumbled through draws with Nicaragua and Jamaica and a March loss to Panama. The model reads it accordingly: 2.06 expected goals for the hosts against just 0.56, a 72.2% Mexico win and only 8.6% for South Africa. Mexico are favoured to open the scoring 72.5% of the time, and the cleanest outcomes on the board are 2-0 (15.4%) and 1-0 (14.5%), with both teams scoring rated a modest 38.1%.

Where the model and the market part company is on the margins of certainty. Polymarket has Mexico priced at a flat 100 cents — effectively no chance for the draw or an upset — whereas the model still leaves almost 28% with South Africa avoiding defeat. On goals the gap flips: the market sits firmly under 2.5, while the model has over 2.5 at a near coin-toss 48.8%. So treat the winner as well-signposted but not sealed, and the total as a genuinely open question rather than the formality the market implies.

review.text:
Mexico opened their World Cup at the Estadio Azteca with a controlled 2-0 win over South Africa, the kind of result the numbers had been pointing at all along. It was a clean continuation of the home side's recent form, and the scoreline landed exactly on the model's single most likely outcome: 2-0 sat top of the projected scores at 15.4 percent, just ahead of 1-0. A 2.06 expected-goals figure for Mexico against 0.56 for South Africa described this game well in advance, and the shutout chimed with a below-even both-teams-to-score read of 38 percent.

This was a good night for both the model and the market, with little to separate them. The model priced Mexico at 72.2 percent to win; Polymarket had them at 69.5 cents — close agreement that South Africa, ranked 60th to Mexico's 15th, would struggle to find a foothold. The locked bracket pick of 1-0 Mexico nailed the result if not the exact margin, and a first-to-score read of 72.5 percent for the hosts captured how the game was always likely to flow. A straightforward call, and a correct one.

preview.text:
South Korea host the Czech Republic in Guadalajara as modest favourites in a matchup the model reads as close. The projected goals — 1.46 to 0.98 — tilt Korean, and that feeds a 47.4% home win against 24.9% for the visitors, with the draw a live 27.7%. The reasoning is partly venue and partly that Korea, ranked 25th to the Czechs' 41st, carry the edge despite trailing on squad value (€139m to €188m). Both sides arrive in form, Korea off back-to-back friendly wins, the Czechs unbeaten across their last nine, which helps explain why the model sees a tight, low-event game: over 2.5 goals lands at just 44%, both teams to score at 48.6%, and the likeliest scores are 1-1 (13.1%) and 1-0 (12.1%).

The gap with the market is stark rather than marginal. Polymarket prices this as a certainty across the board — home win at 100 cents, over 2.5 goals at 100, both teams to score at 100 — where the model treats every one of those as closer to a coin flip or worse. That is not confirmation of either view; it is a genuine disagreement. The model's numbers say this is a contest the Czechs can win or draw, and the market's all-or-nothing pricing leaves no room for that.

review.text:
South Korea opened their Group A campaign with a 2-1 win over the Czech Republic in Guadalajara, turning a tight contest into the kind of narrow home victory the numbers leaned toward without ever promising. The Czechs, the more valuable squad on paper at €188m to Korea's €139m and unbeaten in their previous nine before kickoff, got on the scoresheet but couldn't take a point from the Estadio Akron.

Grade this one a clean hit for the model. It made South Korea favorites at 47.4% to win, with the draw at 27.7% and the Czechs trailing on 24.9%, and the result landed squarely on the right side. The exact 2-1 scoreline was the fifth-likeliest line at 9.1%, and with an xG read of 1.46 to 0.98 the model expected Korea to edge the goal count without a blowout — which is exactly what happened. BTTS came in too, just ahead of the model's near-coin-flip 48.6%. Our locked bracket pick, a 1-1 that called the home result correctly, also graded as a hit. A solid, unflashy night for the projections.

preview.text:
Canada open their home World Cup at BMO Field as heavy favourites, and the model leans into it: 2.36 expected goals to Bosnia's 0.57, a 76.97% home win, and a forecast that Canada strike first 75.8% of the time. The most likely scorelines are all clean sheets — 2-0 at 14.8%, 1-0 at 12.2%, 3-0 at 11.6% — which tells you how the numbers read this: a hosting side with the ranking edge (30th to 65th) and the squad value (€198.65m to €151.6m) controlling territory against opponents the model expects to struggle to score. BTTS sits at just 39.9%.

The disagreement with the market is the real story. Polymarket prices Canada at only 53.5 cents to win, with the draw at 27.5 and Bosnia as live as 20.5 — far above the model's 6.92% for an away win. The market clearly respects Bosnia's qualifying pedigree, including those results against Italy and Wales, in a way the model does not. The two also part on goals: the model has over 2.5 at 56.1% against the market's 42.5. So treat this as genuinely open at the margins — the model sees a comfortable, possibly emphatic Canadian win; the market sees a contest Bosnia can disrupt.

review.text:
Canada and Bosnia and Herzegovina shared a 1-1 draw at BMO Field, a result that quietly punctures the model's confidence. The numbers had painted Canada as overwhelming favourites at home: a 76.97% win probability, an expected goals edge of 2.36 to 0.57, and a three-in-four chance of scoring first. Instead Bosnia, who arrived unbeaten across their last six qualifiers, found the goal that turned a projected rout into a point apiece. The draw was priced at just 16.11%, and both teams scoring was rated only 39.93%, so the way this game played out sat firmly in the model's blind spot.

That makes for an honest miss to grade. The locked bracket pick was 2-0 Canada, the single most likely scoreline at 14.81%, and it landed nowhere near — the actual 1-1 ranked only fifth on the model's board at 7.58%. The market echoed the same heavy lean toward the hosts, so neither view comes out of this looking good. Bosnia's recent form, including draws with Italy and Austria, perhaps deserved more respect than a 6.92% away price allowed. A clear reminder that 77% is not 100%.

preview.text:
The United States open Group D at home in Los Angeles as clear favourites, and the model leans into it: an expected 1.58 goals to Paraguay's 0.82, a 54.6% win probability against 26.4% for the draw and 18.9% for Paraguay. The shape of the matchup explains the gap — a squad valued at €385.65m against Paraguay's €153.65m, a side ranked 16th hosting one ranked 40th, and the memory of a 2-1 US win when these two met as recently as last November. The most likely scoreline is a tight 1-0 at 13.7%, with 1-1 close behind at 12.4%, so this reads as a controlled home win rather than a rout: the US are 59.4% to score first but only 43% of games here clear 2.5 goals.

Where it gets interesting is the moneyline. The market is more cautious about the hosts, pricing them at 47.5 cents against the model's 54.6%, and crediting Paraguay at 24.5 cents versus the model's 18.9%. That is a genuine disagreement worth respecting — the US friendly form is patchy, with losses to Germany, Portugal and Belgium among recent home games. On totals and both teams to score the two sides essentially agree, so the open question is purely how much resistance Paraguay offer.

review.text:
The United States ran out 4-1 winners over Paraguay at SoFi Stadium, a scoreline that comfortably beat the run of pre-match expectation. The model had the hosts as clear favourites at 54.61% with an xG edge of 1.58 to 0.82, and they delivered on the direction emphatically. Both teams scoring was no surprise either, landing right around the 45% the model gave it, and this repeats the pattern from November, when the USA edged Paraguay 2-1 in a friendly.

So the headline call was right: home win backed at 54.61% by the model and 49.5 cents by Polymarket, and a result that arrived. The miss was magnitude. Five goals blew past a market that priced over 3.5 at just 22.12% and over 4.5 at 9.58%; the most likely single scoreline was 1-0 at 13.72%, with the locked bracket pick a cautious 1-1. That graded as a hit on result, but the model never saw a four-goal home haul coming. Credit the lean, dock the totals — the USA were simply more ruthless than the numbers suggested.

preview.text:
This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it as one. Switzerland, ranked 19th and carrying a squad valued at €332.5m against Qatar's €19.93m, are made 80.9% favourites, with the draw at 13.1% and Qatar held to just 6.1%. The expected-goals split tells the same story: 2.78 for the Swiss, 0.67 for Qatar, a gap that flows naturally from recent form. Qatar arrive without a goal in their last two friendlies and have lost four of their last ten, while Switzerland have won five of theirs and put four past both Jordan and Kosovo. The model has the Swiss favoured to score first 77.8% of the time and to lead at the break 60.6%, with 0-2 (12.3%) and 0-3 (11.4%) its likeliest scorelines.

On the result the market barely budges from the model — Polymarket's 80.5% on Switzerland and 13.5% draw are near-identical. The honest disagreement is on goals. The model sees over 2.5 landing 66.9% of the time and both teams scoring at 45.9%; the market is more cautious at 57.5% and 37.5%. That leaves a genuine open question about how freely this game flows, even if nobody doubts who wins it.

review.text:
Qatar held Switzerland to a 1-1 draw at Levi's Stadium, a result that lands among the least likely outcomes on the board. Both sides scored, which the model gave a 45.9% chance, but the bigger story is that Qatar avoided defeat at all. Switzerland, ranked 19th and carrying a squad worth €332.5m against Qatar's €19.93m, were supposed to walk this. The 2.78 expected goals our model handed them never materialised, and the side that lost 0-3 to Tunisia and drew 0-0 with El Salvador last week found a point against far stronger opposition.

This was a clear miss for both the model and the market, and there's no dressing it up. We had Switzerland at 80.86% to win with the draw at just 13.07%; Polymarket sat almost identically at 79.5% and 13.5%. Our locked bracket pick of 0-2 to Switzerland graded out wrong on both score and result. The favourites being throttled to a single goal is exactly the kind of variance an 80% line is supposed to absorb occasionally — today it bit. Credit Qatar; the numbers all pointed the other way.

preview.text:
This is a heavyweight group-stage opener that the model reads as far tighter than the matchup might suggest. Brazil sit sixth in the world to Morocco's eighth, and carry nearly double the squad value at €928.2m against €498.3m, but the expected-goals split is modest: just 1.06 to 0.82 in Brazil's favour. That feeds a cagey set of probabilities — Brazil home at 39.9%, the draw a hefty 33.1% and Morocco still live at 27.0% — with 0-0 actually the single most likely scoreline at 16.0%. Morocco arrive on a serious run, with an AFCON campaign full of clean sheets behind them, and the model clearly respects their organisation: both teams to score is priced at only 37.2%.

Where the model and the market part ways is on Brazil's standing. Polymarket has the favourites at 59.5 cents, almost twenty points clear of the model's read, and trims Morocco to 16.5 against the model's 27.0. The totals tell the same story: the market sees over 2.5 goals at 46.5%, the model far lower at 29.0%. So this is a genuine open question — the market backs Brazilian class to tell, the model expects a low-scoring, closely fought night, and one of them will be graded wrong.

review.text:
Brazil and Morocco shared the points at MetLife Stadium, a 1-1 draw that left the model looking shrewder than the market. Both sides found the net, an outcome the model had pegged at just 37 percent for both-teams-to-score, yet the actual scoreline of 1-1 sat third on its list of likeliest results at 13.9 percent. The model's expected goals of 1.06 to 0.82 pointed to a tight, low-scoring affair, and that is exactly what arrived.

On the result itself, the gap between model and market matters. The model treated this as a genuine open question, giving Brazil only 39.9 percent and the draw a hefty 33.1 percent, while Polymarket priced Brazil as clear favourites at 58.5 cents and the draw at just 24.5. The split point landed on the model's side: Morocco, eighth in the world and unbeaten across their last ten, held the favourites. Our locked bracket pick, a 0-0 Brazil edge, missed on both score and result, predicting a home win where a draw materialised. Credit the model's caution; the market overrated Brazil here.

preview.text:
Scotland arrive as favourites and they look it on paper: ranked 43rd to Haiti's 83rd, with a squad valued at €170.25m against €55.9m, and both sides land in Boston in good touch after 4-0 wins in early June. The model leans Scotland's way without committing to it, projecting 1.58 expected goals to Haiti's 1.19 and pricing the away win at 45.87%, the draw at 26.08% and a Haiti win at 28.05%. That is a clear edge but a long way from a formality, and the scoreline spread reflects it — 1-1 tops the chart at 12.38%, with 0-1 and 1-2 close behind, the picture of a tight game Scotland are expected to nudge rather than dominate.

This is where the market parts company with us. Polymarket has Scotland at 62 cents, a full sixteen points clearer than the model's 45.87%, and trims Haiti to just 16 cents against our 28.05%. The two broadly agree on goals — over 2.5 sits at 52.23% to the market's 57.5%, and both-teams-to-score near the coin-flip at 55.74% versus 53.5% — so the disagreement is purely about how safe Scotland's win is. The model says live underdog; the market says comfortable. Treat that gap as the genuine open question here.

review.text:
Scotland edged Haiti 1-0 at Gillette Stadium, a tight, low-scoring afternoon that gave the favourites their three points without ever turning into the comfortable win their squad value and ranking might suggest. A single goal settled it, the kind of margin that fit a fixture both the model and the market read as Scotland's to lose but not by much.

On the call, this was a solid night. The model made Scotland the pick at 45.9% to win, against 28.1% for Haiti and 26.1% for the draw, and the away result duly landed — and the locked bracket pick had the right winner even if the scoreline was off. Polymarket leaned harder still, pricing Scotland at 62 cents, and that confidence was rewarded. Where the numbers missed was on goals: the model gave the over 2.5 a coin-flip 52.2% and both teams to score 55.7%, yet a clean sheet and a single strike beat both. The exact 0-1 was the second-likeliest scoreline at 9.4%, so even the surprise wasn't really one. A good day for the lean, a quieter one for the goal count.

preview.text:
Played at neutral BC Place but nominally Australia's match, this is a tight one on paper: the model gives the hosts a 1.24 to 1.15 expected-goals edge and prices the three results almost evenly, with Australia at 37.7%, the draw at 29% and Turkey at 33.4%. That reflects a low-scoring, balanced contest — 1-1 is the single likeliest scoreline at 13.7%, over 2.5 goals is closer to a coin-flip at 42.9%, and both teams to score sits at 49.3%. The shape is two sides that grind: Australia have ground out narrow wins over New Zealand, Canada and Cameroon, while Turkey arrive on a strong run, beating Venezuela and thrashing FYR Macedonia 4-0.

The real story is the gap between the model and the market. Polymarket sees this very differently, making Turkey clear favourites at 57.5 cents and pushing Australia all the way down to 17.5 — far below the model's 37.7%. That's a chasm, and the market has reasons the model doesn't fully weigh: Turkey rank 22nd to Australia's 27th, and their squad is valued at €473.7m against just €77.45m. On totals the two broadly agree, but on who wins this is a genuine open question, and the disagreement is stark.

review.text:
Australia took this one in front of a Vancouver crowd, winning 2-0 to claim the points against a Turkey side that arrived with all the pre-match billing. The two goals split the model's totals neatly: over 1.5 was favoured at 69.7% and landed, while over 2.5 (42.9%) did not, a low-event game roughly in line with the model's modest 1.24-1.15 expected-goals read.

This was a clean win for the model over the market. Our numbers framed the match as a near coin-flip with Australia nudged ahead at 37.7% to Turkey's 33.4%, and the locked bracket pick correctly called a home result. Polymarket saw it very differently, pricing Turkey as firm favourites at 56.5 cents and writing Australia off at just 17.5 — a reading that looked reasonable on squad value, with Turkey's €473.7m dwarfing Australia's €77.5m, but proved badly wrong on the night. Credit where due: the model trusted Australia's recent home form and resisted the market's lean. The honest caveat is that a 37.7% favourite winning is no triumph of certainty, only a sensible call paying off.

preview.text:
This is about as lopsided as a World Cup group game gets. Germany, ranked 10th and carrying a squad worth €947m against Curaçao's €25.78m, arrive in Houston on a near-perfect run — nine wins in their last ten, including a 6-0 over Slovakia in qualifying and a 2-1 friendly defeat of the USA last week. Curaçao, 82nd in the world, have looked solid in CONCACAF but porous against bigger sides, shipping four to Scotland and five to Australia in recent friendlies. The model reads it accordingly: 3.5 expected goals for Germany against just 0.55, an 89.95% home win, and a clear favourite to score first at 84.8%. The likeliest scorelines are all routs — 3-0, 4-0 and 2-0 cluster at the top.

Where it gets interesting is the margin of disagreement. Polymarket is even more bullish on Germany, pricing the win at 94 cents to the model's 90, and leans harder into goals too — 82.5% on over 2.5 versus the model's 76.9%, and 45.5 cents against 38.1 on over 4.5. The one place the model pushes back is both teams to score, at 41.2% against the market's 32.5%, a hint it credits Curaçao with more of a puncher's chance than the traders do.

review.text:
Germany turned a routine group-stage favourite's job into a rout, putting seven past Curaçao and conceding once for a 7-1 win at the NRG Stadium. The result honoured the underlying gap the numbers screamed about all week: a squad valued at €947m against €25.78m, the world's 10th-ranked side against the 82nd. Both teams found the net, vindicating the model's 41.2% both-teams-to-score read, and the locked bracket pick of a home win landed cleanly.

On the headline, both the model and the market were emphatically right: the model priced Germany at 89.95% and Polymarket leaned even harder at 93.85 cents, and the favourite duly delivered. Where the call missed was the scale of it. Our expected goals of 3.5 for Germany pointed to a comfortable win, not a seven-goal avalanche, and the totals lines reflected that caution — over 4.5 goals sat at just 38.11% and over 5.5 at only 22.29%. Seven goals sailed past both. The pre-match top scores topped out at 5-0; nobody had this margin. A correct direction, then, but a serious under-read on Germany's ceiling.

preview.text:
This is the dossier's most interesting disagreement, and it sits right in the result market. On paper Netherlands carry the pedigree — seventh in the world, a squad valued at €754m against Japan's €271m — but the model sees a coin flip leaning slightly the wrong way for the favourites. It splits the expected goals almost evenly at 1.21 to 1.26 and actually makes Japan marginal favourites, 36.9% to win against 34.6% for the Dutch, with the draw at 28.5%. Japan's record backs that read: wins over England, Scotland and Brazil dot a long unbeaten stretch, while Netherlands lost at home to Algeria as recently as 8 June. It shapes up as a tight, low-event game — 0-0 is the fourth-likeliest score at 9.1%, and 1-1 leads the board at 13.5%.

That is where Polymarket parts company with the numbers. The market prices Netherlands at 47.5 cents, a clear favourite, and pushes Japan all the way down to 26.5 — almost the mirror image of the model's lean. Both sides agree this should be a cagey watch: over 2.5 goals sits at 44.9% on the model and 47.5 in the market, with both-teams-to-score around the coin-flip mark. The totals are settled; the winner is a genuine open question, and the model thinks the market is underrating Japan.

review.text:
Netherlands and Japan shared four goals in Dallas, a 2-2 draw that landed squarely in the model's wheelhouse on shape if not on scoreline. Both-teams-to-score was effectively a coin flip at 51 percent and duly came in, and the near-identical expected goals — 1.21 for the Dutch, 1.26 for Japan — captured a game with nothing between two sides who couldn't be separated. Japan, ranked 18th to the Netherlands' 7th and worth barely a third as much by squad value, again showed the durability that built their unbeaten recent run, refusing to lose to a far more expensive opponent.

The draw itself was the model's second-most-likely result at 28.5 percent, but four goals tested it: over 3.5 was priced at just 23.7 percent, so the scoring was a touch above expectation. The market leaned harder on the Netherlands, pricing them at 47 cents against the model's more cautious 35 percent, and that confidence didn't pay — honours even is a mild miss for Polymarket and a fair call for the model. Our locked bracket pick of 1-1 to Japan got the tightness right but the winner and scoreline wrong; graded an honest miss.

preview.text:
This is a low-scoring chess match by both readings, but the model leans harder into that than the market does. Ecuador's defensive solidity — clean sheets against Canada, Paraguay and a 1-0 win over Argentina dot their recent form — pairs with an Ivory Coast attack the model rates at just 0.69 expected goals to 0.83 for the visitors. That edge tips Ecuador to a 35.5% win against the Ivorians' 27%, but the model's real conviction is the draw at 37.6%, and the single most likely result is a goalless 0-0 at 22.5%. Ecuador are favoured to score first (42.5%) and the contest is expected to be cagey from the start, with a level half-time the runaway favourite at 57.3%.

Where model and market part ways is the goals total. The model sees under 2.5 as comfortably the bet, pricing over 2.5 at just 19.6% and both teams to score at 28.7%. Polymarket is far more open on both, marking over 2.5 at 32.5 cents and BTTS at 41.5 cents — a meaningful gap that says the market expects a livelier game than the numbers do. The moneyline is closer to agreement, with Polymarket at 37.5 away, 32.5 draw and 29.5 home. Treat the goals question as genuinely open.

review.text:
Ivory Coast got the win their underdog status didn't predict, edging Ecuador 1-0 in Philadelphia to claim all three points in this group E opener. The single goal was enough against an Ecuador side that had drawn five of its previous ten and arrived ranked eleven places higher at 23rd in the world.

This one goes down as a miss for both the model and the market. The model leaned away from an Ivory Coast win entirely, pricing them at just 26.97% with the draw the most likely single outcome at 37.57%, and its locked bracket pick of a 0-0 with Ecuador edging it on aggregate value was wrong on both score and result. Polymarket was no closer, making Ecuador clear favourites at 39.5 cents against Ivory Coast's 26.5. The lone consolation is that the exact 1-0 scoreline was the model's third-likeliest result at 14.41%, behind only 0-0 and 0-1, so the low-scoring shape was well read even as the winner wasn't. Days like this are the reminder that a 26.97% chance still comes in better than one time in four.

preview.text:
Sweden go in as modest favourites at the Estadio BBVA, and the gap is real but narrow. The model gives them 1.22 expected goals to Tunisia's 0.96, which translates to a 41.4 percent win, with the draw at 30.2 and Tunisia at 28.4. That is the profile of a tight, low-scoring group game rather than a mismatch, and the scoreline distribution backs it up: 1-1 leads the board at 13.9 percent, just ahead of 1-0 at 13.1 and a 0-0 at 11.9. On paper Sweden hold the edge in squad value, 406m euros to roughly 70m, but recent form offers little comfort to either side, so a cagey night with under 2.5 goals landing 62.6 percent of the time feels the honest read.

Where it gets interesting is the disagreement with the market. Polymarket prices Sweden at 50.5 cents, a clear nine points above the model's 41.4, and trims both the draw and Tunisia accordingly. The market also leans for goals, pricing over 2.5 at 44.5 against the model's 37.4 and both teams to score at 48.5 versus 44.4. So the bettors see a more comfortable, more open Sweden win; the model sees a coin-flip-adjacent grind. Treat it as a genuinely open question.

review.text:
Sweden buried Tunisia 5-1 in Monterrey, a result that lands at the very edge of what anyone had drawn up. The model gave over 4.5 goals a 7.1 percent chance and over 5.5 just 2.4 percent, so six goals in a single game was firmly in the tail. Sweden's expected goals of 1.22 became five on the night, while Tunisia's 0.96 at least produced a consolation. After a run that included a 0-5 loss to Belgium and a 0-1 to Austria, Tunisia's defensive frailty showed up again here.

On the call itself, both views had Sweden as favourites and both were right about the winner: the model priced the home side at 41.4 percent, Polymarket more bullish at 50.5 cents, and the locked bracket pick of a 1-1 home result counts as a hit on result if not on margin. Where everyone missed badly was the scale. Nobody saw a five-goal Swedish haul coming, with a draw rated 30.2 percent and the most likely scoreline a humble 1-1 at 13.9 percent. Right team, wildly understated emphasis.

preview.text:
Spain arrive ranked second in the world with a squad valued at €1.22bn, a gulf over Cape Verde's €54.5m and the 67-place ranking gap that frames this one as a straightforward mismatch. The model agrees: it projects 2.98 expected goals for Spain against just 0.41, and makes them 88.1% to win with the draw a distant 9.3% and a Cape Verde upset barely registering at 2.7%. Spain are 84.9% to open the scoring and 67.2% to lead at the break, and the most likely scorelines cluster around 2-0, 3-0 and 4-0. None of that should surprise after a qualifying run that included 6-0 and 4-0 results, though recent friendlies — a 1-1 with Iraq, a goalless draw with Egypt — were scratchier.

The market backs the favourite even harder, pricing Spain at 91.5 cents to the model's 88.1, and it leans into the goals too: Polymarket has over 2.5 at 72.5 cents where the model sits at 65.7%, and over 3.5 essentially a coin flip at 50.5 against the model's 43.8%. The honest read is that both see the same result; the open question is purely the margin, where the model is a shade more cautious about a blowout.

Belgium arrive as clear favourites, and the shape of the matchup explains why. The ninth-ranked side carry a squad valued at €547.5m against Egypt's €116.48m, and they come in scorching, beating Tunisia 5-0 and Croatia 2-0 in their last two friendlies. The model reads it as control rather than a procession: 1.5 expected goals for Belgium against 0.71 for Egypt, a 55.7% win probability, and a healthy 28.3% chance of the draw. Egypt are no pushover defensively — three clean sheets in their last ten, including a goalless draw away to Spain — and that shows in a modest 39.9% both-teams-to-score figure and a most-likely scoreline of just 1-0 at 16.0%.

The interesting friction is with the market. Polymarket prices Belgium at 61.5 cents to win, noticeably bolder than the model's 55.7%, and it expects a more open game: over 2.5 goals trades at 49.5 cents against the model's 37.8%, with both-teams-to-score at 48.5 versus 39.9%. So the disagreement is real and worth flagging — the market backs Belgium harder and bets on goals, while the model sees a tighter, lower-scoring win. Egypt's resilience is the genuine open question between the two views.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Uruguay sit 17th in the world to Saudi Arabia's 61st, and the gulf in squad value — €359.3m against €40.68m — is even starker. The Dixon-Coles numbers follow suit: 1.57 expected goals for Uruguay, just 0.43 for the Saudis, with the South Americans favoured at 65.6% and Saudi Arabia given only a 9.3% chance of the win. Uruguay are also overwhelmingly likely to open the scoring (67.7%), and the model's most probable outcomes — 0-1 at 20.8% and 0-2 at 16.7% — paint the picture of a controlled away win rather than a goal-fest. A 0-0 still rates 14%, reflecting Uruguay's recent run of blanks against Algeria, Mexico and Chile.

Market and model agree closely on the result — Polymarket has Uruguay at 66.5 cents, essentially level with the model — so the genuine disagreement is on goals. The model sees this as a low-scoring affair, pricing over 2.5 at 32.4% and both teams to score at just 28.1%. The market is markedly more open, paying 45.5 cents on over 2.5 and 41.5 on BTTS. If you trust the model's read on Saudi Arabia's shot-shy attack, the unders and the no-BTTS look like the value.

Iran come into Los Angeles as clear favourites and the numbers back the framing: ranked 21st to New Zealand's 85th, with a goal model that expects 1.46 from Iran against 1.01 for the All Whites. That edge is more about attacking output than control — Iran's form has been the steadier of the two, and the model makes them 53.8% to open the scoring. New Zealand arrive on a grim run, beaten 4-0 by Haiti and 1-0 by England in their last two, and the model duly leans Iranian: 46.9% for the win, 27.6% for the draw, 25.5% for an upset. The likeliest single scorelines are tight ones, 1-1 at 13.1% and 1-0 at 11.7%, so a comfortable evening is far from guaranteed.

Where model and market part ways is instructive. Polymarket is firmer on Iran at 50.5 cents and shades New Zealand down to 21.5, while our model keeps the away side live at better than one in four. The bigger split is on goals: the model sees this as the more open game, pricing over 2.5 at 44.9% against the market's 38.5%, and both teams to score at 49.5% versus 43.5%. Treat both the favourite's margin and the goal count as genuinely open.

France open Group I as clear favourites against Senegal, and the numbers behind that are straightforward: top-ranked, a squad valued at €1.52bn against Senegal's €478m, and an attack the model expects to generate 1.42 goals to 0.86. That edge shows up across the board — France are 55.6% to score first, the most likely scoreline is 1-0 at 13.9%, and a low-event game is in play, with over 2.5 goals only a 39.8% shot and both teams scoring rated 44.3%. Senegal arrive in decent form themselves, with an Africa Cup of Nations run that included wins over Egypt and Mali, but the model frames this as France controlling without running away with it.

The interesting gap is on the moneyline. Our model makes France 49.6% to win, with the draw a live 28.3% and Senegal 22%; Polymarket is far bolder, pricing France at 66.5c and shrinking Senegal to just 12.5c. That is a wide disagreement — the market sees something close to a routine French win, while the model treats this as a genuine contest where one Senegal goal changes the texture. On totals the two are closer, though the market still leans higher, pricing over 2.5 at 50.5c against our 39.8%.

This is a mismatch by almost any measure. Norway arrive ranked 31st to Iraq's 57th, with a squad valued at €589.9m against €21.2m, and the model's expected goals tell the same story: 2.0 for Norway, just 0.56 for Iraq. That gap leans on recent scoring form — Norway have racked up 4-1, 5-0 and 11-1 results in European qualifying — and it pushes them to a 71% win probability, with Iraq at 8.9% and the draw at 20%. The model has Norway first to score 71.7% of the time and likeliest scorelines of 0-2 and 0-1, a portrait of Iraq defending deep and chasing the game.

Where it gets interesting is the disagreement with the market. Polymarket prices Norway far harder at 82.5 cents, trimming Iraq to just 5.5 and the draw to 12.5 — the model is notably more willing to allow an upset or a stalemate than the betting crowd. The same split shows up on goals: the market sees over 2.5 at 61.5 cents while the model sits at 47%, essentially a coin flip. So the lean is Norway, comfortably, but the model thinks both the certainty and the goal glut are being overpriced. That's the genuine open question here.

Argentina go into Kansas City as heavy favourites, and the shape of this one is straightforward: the model's expected goals read 1.74 to 0.62, a gap that tracks the gulf in pedigree between the world's third-ranked side and a squad valued at €256.9m against Argentina's €782.5m. That feeds a 64% home win, with the draw at 24% and Algeria handed just a 12% shout. Argentina are tipped to score first 66% of the time and the favourite scorelines all carry their fingerprints — 1-0 (16%) and 2-0 (14%) lead the board, with 1-1 (11%) the best Algeria can realistically hope for. This looks like a controlled, low-volume win rather than a romp: under 2.5 goals is the model's lean at 58%, and both teams scoring sits at just 38%.

Where the market pushes back is on the margin of certainty. Polymarket prices Argentina at 70.5 cents, well clear of the model's 64%, and shaves Algeria to 9.5. The market also expects a touch more action, pricing over 2.5 at 48.5 cents against the model's 41.8%. None of it is dramatic, but the disagreement is real: traders see a slightly safer, slightly livelier Argentina win than the numbers do.

A clear favourite-versus-underdog shape at Levi's Stadium: Austria sit 24th in the world to Jordan's 63rd, and the squad values tell the same story, €242.2m against €20.3m. The model leans firmly Austrian, projecting 1.95 expected goals to 0.78 and making them 64.6% to win, with the draw at 21.9% and a Jordan upset just 13.6%. Austria are expected to set the tone early — 66.5% to score first — though a tight first half is plausible, the model splitting half-time honours almost evenly between an Austrian lead (46.3%) and the draw (40.1%). The most likely scorelines are low and controlled: 2-0 at 12.4%, 1-0 at 12.3%, with over 2.5 goals a genuine coin-flip at 51.4% and both teams scoring only 46.8%.

The interesting gap is on the moneyline. Polymarket prices Austria at 72.5 cents, a clear step above the model's 64.6%, and shades Jordan's chances down to 9.5% against the model's 13.6%. So the market sees a more comfortable Austrian afternoon than the numbers do. On goals the two broadly agree — the market's 56.5% over 2.5 is a touch higher than the model's 51.4% — leaving the size of Austria's edge, not its direction, as the open question here.

This is a clear favourite against a stubborn underdog. Portugal sit fifth in the world to DR Congo's 46th, and the squad-value gap is vast — €1,010m against €143.9m — so the model frames it as a question of whether Portugal can break down a side that defends well rather than whether they win. The projected scoreline reads 1.68 to 0.51, and the most likely results are all low and one-sided: 1-0 at 18.4%, 2-0 at 15.8%, with a goalless draw third at 11.7%. Portugal are 67.8% to score first and the model gives them a 65.8% win, leaning on DR Congo's recent miserliness — a 0-0 with Denmark, plus shutout wins over Jamaica and Cameroon.

The disagreement is one of degree, not direction. Polymarket prices Portugal at 76.5 cents to win against the model's 65.8%, treating this as closer to a formality. The same split runs through the goals markets: the model sees over 2.5 at just 37.5% while the market sits at 56.5%, and BTTS at 32.9% against 41 cents. In short, the market expects a more open, higher-scoring afternoon than the numbers do — worth watching if DR Congo's defensive record holds.

England arrive in Dallas as the heavy favourite, and the model leaves little doubt why. A squad valued at €1,360m against Croatia's €387.3m, a number four world ranking against eleven, and a recent run that includes back-to-back clean sheets over Serbia all push expected goals to 1.58 for England and just 0.69 for Croatia. That gap drives a 58.3 percent home win, with the draw at 26.2 percent and Croatia given only 15.5 percent. England are favoured to open the scoring 62 percent of the time, and the most likely scorelines are tellingly cagey — 1-0 at 15.8 percent, 2-0 at 12.9 — pointing to control rather than a goal rush.

This is a low-event projection: over 2.5 goals lands at just 39.6 percent and both teams to score at 40.1 percent, consistent with a side that wins by suffocation. The market broadly agrees on the favourite but trims the edges, pricing England at 56.5 cents and giving Croatia a slightly warmer 18.5 against the model's 15.5. The clearer disagreement is on goals: Polymarket has both teams to score at 47.5 cents and over 2.5 at 44.5, noticeably above the model. Whether Croatia's attack can puncture England's defence is the open question here.

Despite sitting 41 places below Panama in the FIFA rankings, Ghana open this Group L tie as home favourites at BMO Field, and the squad-value gap explains why: their players are worth €234.6m against Panama's €34.55m. The model leans the same way, projecting 1.36 expected goals to 1.03 and giving Ghana a 43.6% win against 27.9% for Panama, with the draw close behind at 28.6%. The recent form lines complicate the picture, though — Ghana's friendly run reads poorly, including a 5-1 thrashing by Austria, while Panama have been competitive, beating South Africa and pushing through CONCACAF qualifying. This shapes up as a tight, modestly-scoring contest: the most likely scorelines are 1-1 (13.5%), 1-0 (11.8%) and a goalless draw (9.8%), and the model splits 0-0 to over-three-goal outcomes almost evenly, with over 2.5 priced at just 42.7%.

There is no real argument with Polymarket here. The market has Ghana at 44.5 cents, the draw at 27.5 and Panama at 27.5, a near-mirror of the model's read, and the totals agree just as tightly — over 2.5 at 41.5 against the model's 42.7%, both-teams-to-score at 48 against 48.4%. When model and market line up this cleanly, the honest call is that the price is fair.

This is a clear favourite-against-outsider setup at the Azteca, and the numbers reflect it. Colombia sit 13th in the world to Uzbekistan's 50th, with a squad valued at €302m against €85m, and the model hands them a 1.5 expected-goals edge over the Uzbeks' 0.63. That translates to a 57.98% win probability for Colombia, 27.11% for the draw and just 14.91% for Uzbekistan. The shape is low-scoring rather than a rout: under 2.5 goals is the likelier outcome at 64%, both teams scoring is only a 36.88% shot, and the model's four most probable scorelines are all tight — 0-1, 0-2, 0-0 and 1-1. Colombia are first to score 61.78% of the time, but a goalless first half (46.89%) is the single most likely halftime state.

Where the model and market part company is on degree. Polymarket prices Colombia at 70.5 cents to win, well clear of the model's 58%, and trims Uzbekistan to 9.5 and the draw to 20.5. The market also expects more goals, pricing over 2.5 at 49 cents against the model's 35.95%. So the disagreement isn't about who wins — it's whether Colombia win comfortably and openly, or grind through a cagey, tight afternoon. Treat that as genuinely open.

The Czech Republic open Group A in Atlanta as clear favourites, and the numbers explain why. Their squad is valued at €188m against South Africa's €49m, they sit 19 places higher in the FIFA ranking, and they arrive on a strong run — five wins in their last six, including draws held against Denmark and Croatia. The model reflects that edge without overstating it: 1.32 expected goals to 0.85, a 47.2% win probability against South Africa's 23.3%, with the draw a live 29.6%. This shapes up as a controlled rather than emphatic favourite's afternoon. The Czechs are likeliest to draw first blood (53.6%), but at half-time the model actually favours parity at 47.7%, hinting at a slow build rather than an early breakaway.

That points to a tight, low-scoring game: under 2.5 goals is the bet, with over 2.5 only 36.9% and 1-0, 1-1 and 0-0 the three most probable scorelines. Here the market pushes back hardest. Polymarket prices the Czechs at 57.5 cents to South Africa's 17.5, a notably bolder favourite than the model's, and leans to more goals — over 2.5 at 44.5 cents against the model's 36.9%. On the win line and the total, then, the market sees a clearer, busier Czech victory than the model will endorse.

Switzerland are the clear favourites at SoFi Stadium, and the gap is built on more than the rankings — 19th against 65th, a squad valued at €332.5m against €151.6m, and a model expecting them to carry the play. The numbers point to a controlled Swiss performance: 2.06 expected goals to Bosnia's 0.62, a 71.2% chance of scoring first, and a moneyline reading of 70.6% home. That tilt comes off a strong Swiss qualifying run, with four-goal wins over Kosovo and Sweden, while Bosnia arrive unbeaten in their last competitive run but draw-heavy, with stalemates against Macedonia and Panama in their warm-ups. The model's top scorelines — 2-0 at 14.5%, 1-0 at 13.6% — suggest a tight, low-scoring win rather than a rout, with over 2.5 goals essentially a coin flip at 50.3%.

Where the model and market part ways is on how alive this is for Bosnia. Polymarket prices the away side at 16.5 cents and the draw at 23.5, against the model's leaner 9.7% and 19.7%. In other words, traders see meaningfully more upset and stalemate risk than the goal model does. That disagreement is worth respecting: Bosnia's recent results say they are awkward to break down, so treat the Swiss favouritism as strong but not settled.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model reads it that way. Canada, ranked 30th to Qatar's 55th and carrying a squad valued near ten times higher (€198.65m to €19.93m), get an expected 3.18 goals at BC Place against just 0.61. That feeds an 86.3% win probability, with the draw a distant 9.8% and a Qatar upset priced at under 4%. The shape fits both teams' recent form: Qatar arrive on a grim run — a 0-3 loss to Tunisia, defeats to Palestine and Zimbabwe — while Canada are unbeaten across their last five friendlies, if often by narrow margins. The model leans on a fast Canadian start too, giving them a 66% lead at the break and an 82% chance of scoring first. The likeliest scorelines cluster around 3-0, 2-0 and 4-0.

Where the numbers part ways is on degree, not direction. Polymarket has Canada at 74.5 cents, a clear step below the model's 86%, and inflates the draw to 17.5 against the model's 9.8%. The gap widens on goals: the model makes over 2.5 a 73% shot, the market only 48.5 cents. So both sides agree Canada should win — the open question is whether they win as emphatically as the model insists.

Mexico get this group A opener at home in Guadalajara, and the model treats them as clear but not overwhelming favourites: 1.53 expected goals to South Korea's 0.97, which translates to a 49.7% win probability against 23.4% for the visitors and a 26.9% draw. The shape is a familiar one — a higher-ranked host (15th to South Korea's 25th) leaning on home advantage and the better recent run, including a 5-1 win over Serbia this month, while Korea arrive with a streakier profile and the reminder that they held Mexico 2-2 away just last September. Mexico are favoured to open the scoring at 55.8%, and the most likely scoreline is a tight 1-1 at 12.8%, just ahead of 1-0.

On the moneyline the model and Polymarket are essentially holding hands — 49.7% versus 48.5% on Mexico, the draw and away prices a fraction apart — so there's no edge to chase on the result. The interesting gap is on goals. The model sees a livelier game: 71.9% for over 1.5 against the market's 65.5%, and 45.7% for over 2.5 where Polymarket sits at 40%. Both-teams-to-score is close at 49.3% to 46.5%. If you believe the model, the value is in the over.

On home soil at Lumen Field, the model frames this as a clear but not commanding United States edge: 1.51 expected goals to Australia's 1.05, which feeds a 47.4% home win, a 27.0% draw and a 25.6% Socceroos upset. The lean rests on the obvious gap in resources — a €385.65m squad against €77.45m, FIFA's 16 against 27 — and on recent history, with the USA having beaten Australia 1-2 in Sydney last October. The scoreline distribution is tight and low-scoring: 1-1 tops the board at 12.9%, ahead of 1-0 at 11.1%, with both teams to score essentially a coin flip at 51.2% and over 2.5 priced at 47.2%.

The interesting friction is on the moneyline. Polymarket has the USA at 56.5 cents, a full nine points clear of the model's 47.4%, and trims both the draw (24.5 vs 27.0%) and the away side (20.5 vs 25.6%). In short, the market backs the hosts harder than our numbers do, treating Australia's chances as thinner than the model will allow. On totals the two agree almost exactly — over 2.5 at 46.5 cents against 47.2% — so the genuine open question here is purely how much credit the Socceroos deserve, not how many goals get scored.

Morocco arrive in Boston as the clear class side, ranked 8th in the world against Scotland's 43rd, with a squad valued at €498m to €170m. The model reflects that gap bluntly: Morocco are 65.8% to win, Scotland just 10.1%, with the draw at 24.1%. The expected goals tell the same story, 1.67 to 0.50, and Morocco's recent form backs it up — they reached this point on the back of an unbeaten Africa Cup of Nations run, beating Senegal 3-0 and shutting out Nigeria. Scotland's 4-0 friendly win over Bolivia flatters a team that lost to both Japan and Ivory Coast in March. The model's single likeliest outcome is a 0-1 Morocco win at 18.6%, with Morocco favoured to score first 67.8% of the time.

Where it gets interesting is the disagreement with the market. Polymarket prices Scotland far more generously at 22.5 cents, more than double the model's read, and trims Morocco to 49.5. The market also leans higher-scoring: both teams to score sits at 47.5 cents versus the model's 32.4%, and over 2.5 goals at 40.5 against 36.9%. So the shape is agreed — Morocco on top — but how comfortably, and how open, is a genuine open question.

This is as lopsided as group-stage football gets. Brazil, ranked 6th with a squad valued at €928m, host a Haiti side ranked 83rd and worth €55.9m, and the model reflects that gulf: it projects 3.35 goals for Brazil against 0.58, and makes them 88.3% to win with the draw a distant 8.5% and a Haiti upset just 3.2%. The scoreline distribution is a parade of clean-sheet wins — 3-0 (12.3%), 2-0 (11%) and 4-0 (10.3%) head the list — and Brazil are 83.4% to open the scoring. There is decent over-3.5 value too, priced at 55.3% by the model, with over-2.5 a comfortable 75.2%.

Where it gets interesting is at the edges. The market is firmer on Brazil than we are, pricing them at 90.5 cents to our 88.3, and shaving the draw to 6.1 cents. The bigger gap is both teams to score: the model sees 42.7%, the market only 35.5% — a genuine disagreement worth flagging given Haiti's recent form, which includes a 4-0 win over New Zealand and a 3-3 draw at Costa Rica. They can find the net, even if keeping Brazil out looks beyond them.

Turkey come in ranked 22nd and on a strong run, eight wins in their last ten including qualifying victories over Romania and Kosovo and a 2-2 draw away to Spain, while Paraguay sit 40th with a more mixed record of friendly results either side of solid CONMEBOL qualifying. That gap, plus the squad-value chasm of €473.7m to €153.65m, is why the model leans Turkey but only narrowly: home win 40.4%, draw 30.4%, Paraguay 29.2%. The expected goals tell the same cautious story, 1.2 to 0.98, pointing to a tight, low-scoring game rather than a mismatch. The single most likely score is 1-1 at 14.0%, with 1-0 and 0-0 close behind, and over 2.5 goals is only a 37.1% shot.

Where model and market part ways is on the margins. Polymarket prices Turkey a touch higher at 43.5 cents and trims Paraguay to 26.5, so it sees the favourite as the clearer side; the model keeps Paraguay live as a genuine third of the time. The market also expects more goals, pricing both teams to score at 49.5 cents against the model's 44.2% and over 2.5 at 41 to 37. Treat the favourite's edge and the goal count as the real open questions here.

The shape here is a clear favourite against a side arriving in poor form. The Netherlands sit seventh in the world to Sweden's 38th, with a squad valued at €754.2m against €406.1m, and the model leans heavily on that gap: it projects 2.1 expected goals for the hosts to just 0.96 for Sweden, and makes the Dutch 63.1% to win in Houston. Form backs the read. The Netherlands lost only once in their last ten and battered Finland, Malta and Lithuania 4-0 in qualifying, while Sweden have collapsed badly, taking only a couple of recent wins amid heavy defeats to Switzerland (1-4 and 0-2) and Kosovo. The model has the Netherlands 65.1% to score first and 2-0 as the single likeliest scoreline at 10.3%.

The market broadly agrees but trims the edges. Polymarket prices the Dutch at 59.5 cents to the model's 63.1%, nudging the draw and Sweden slightly longer in the model's eyes. The sharper disagreement is on goals: the model sees Over 2.5 at 59% against the market's 51.5 cents, and both-teams-to-score at 54.6% versus 50.5. So the genuine open question isn't who wins but how open it gets — the model expects more goals than the market is willing to pay for.

Germany arrive in Toronto on a near-flawless run, nine wins in their last ten with the only blemish a 0-2 loss in Slovakia, and a squad valued at €947m against Ivory Coast's €522m. But the gap is narrower than the FIFA rankings (10th versus 34th) suggest: the Ivorians have won eight of ten themselves, including a 2-1 away win over France this June. The model reads it as a clear but far from settled favourite's game, projecting 1.42 expected goals for Germany against 0.97 — enough for a 46.7% win probability, with the draw live at 28.1% and Ivory Coast holding a real 25.2% puncher's chance. The likeliest scorelines are tight: 1-1 at 13.3%, 1-0 at 12.4%, and a goalless draw still 9.8%.

This is where the model and the market part company sharply. Polymarket prices Germany at 63.5 cents, treating the win as close to a formality, while the model's 46.7% says it is anything but. The market also leans higher-scoring, pricing over 2.5 goals at 52.5 cents against the model's 42.8%. The two only truly agree on both teams scoring, near-identical at 48 cents and 47.7%. Treat Germany's margin, not the result, as the genuine open question.

This is a mismatch on paper and the model treats it as one. Ecuador sit 23rd in FIFA's ranking to Curaçao's 82nd, with a squad valued at €368.7m against €25.78m, and the goal model hands the favourites a 2.06 expected-goals edge to just 0.39. The most likely scorelines are tightly bunched around a low-scoring home win — 2-0 at 18.3%, 1-0 at 17.5%, 3-0 at 12.6% — and both teams to score is only a 28.4% shot.

Market and model largely agree on the winner: Polymarket prices Ecuador at 80.5 cents to the model's 76.7%, a modest lean toward the favourite. The clearer disagreement is on goals. The market sees this game as livelier, pricing over 2.5 at 57 cents while the model has it at just 44.2%, and BTTS at 35.5 cents against the model's 28.4%. If you trust the numbers, the value sits with the unders and a tighter, lower-scoring afternoon in Kansas City.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it like one. Japan arrive ranked 18th with a squad valued at €270.85m against Tunisia's €69.95m, and recent form sharpens the picture: Japan have beaten England, Scotland and Iceland 1-0 apiece, while Tunisia were taken apart 0-5 by Belgium and lost 0-1 to Austria in their last two warm-ups. The model projects 1.61 expected goals for Japan against just 0.56 for Tunisia, and makes Japan 62.6% to win with the draw at 25.3% and a Tunisia win down at 12.1%. The single likeliest scoreline is 0-1 at 17.9%, followed by 0-2 at 14.8%, and Japan are 65.3% to open the scoring.

The market broadly agrees but shades a touch more cautious on Japan, pricing them at 58.5 cents to the model's 62.6%, and nudging Tunisia up to 15.5 cents. The sharper gap is on goals: Polymarket sees both teams scoring at 45.5%, well clear of the model's 34.7%, and leans slightly over on totals, with over 2.5 at 41.5 cents versus the model's 36.8%. That leaves a genuine question over whether Tunisia find the net at all — the model expects a tighter, lower-scoring Japan win than the market is bracing for.

This is about as lopsided as group-stage fixtures get. Spain arrive ranked second in the world with a squad valued at €1.22bn against Saudi Arabia's €40.68m, and the form lines underline the gap: Spain are unbeaten across their last ten with qualifying wins like 6-0 in Türkiye and 4-0 in Georgia, while Saudi Arabia have lost five of their last ten, including a 0-4 home defeat to Egypt. The model reads it accordingly, handing Spain an expected 2.84 goals to 0.4 and an 86.9% win probability against just 2.9% for the Saudis, with a draw a remote 10.2%. Spain are 84.1% to open the scoring, and the scoreline distribution clusters tightly around clean sheets — 2-0, 3-0, 1-0 and 4-0 are the four likeliest results, which is why both-teams-to-score sits at only 31%.

There is almost nothing to argue about here, and the market agrees. Polymarket prices Spain at 87.5 cents to the model's 86.9%, with the draw and the Saudi win line up similarly close. The totals match too: over 2.5 goals is 62.7% in the model against 65 cents in the market, and over 1.5 lands at 83.5% versus 85.5. Model and market are effectively shaking hands.

Belgium arrive in Los Angeles as comfortable favourites, and the shape of this one is the classic mismatch of resources: a squad valued at €547.5m against Iran's €32.05m, ninth in the world against twenty-first. The model leans hard into that, projecting 1.8 expected goals for Belgium against 0.83 for Iran and pricing the home win at 60%, the draw at 24% and an Iran upset at just 16%. Belgium have scored freely in the build-up — 5-0 over Tunisia, 2-0 in Croatia — and the model makes them 63% to open the scoring. The most likely scorelines all favour them, with 1-0 (12.5%) and 2-0 (11.7%) leading the board.

This is not expected to be a goal feast, though. The model sits almost exactly on the fence for over 2.5 goals at 48.8%, and both teams to score is a coin-flip at 47.5%, reflecting Iran's capacity to defend deep and frustrate. The market is a touch more bullish on Belgium, pricing the home win at 68.5 cents against the model's 60% and trimming Iran to 11.5 cents. That gap is the one genuine disagreement worth flagging: the model gives Iran a little more daylight than the traders do.

Uruguay arrive ranked 17th with a squad valued near €359m against Cape Verde's €55m, and the model reads the gap exactly as a mismatch of class against organisation. Expected goals split 1.65 to 0.44, which is why the home side prices at 67% to win, against a 24% draw and just under 9% for Cape Verde. Uruguay are tipped to open the scoring 69% of the time, and the most likely scorelines are tight and one-sided — 1-0 at 20%, 2-0 at 17%, with a goalless draw still third on the list at 13%. This is a control job rather than a shootout: Cape Verde have kept clean sheets in qualifying but conceded in patches, and Uruguay's recent friendlies have been low-scoring and cagey.

The market broadly agrees on the result — Polymarket has Uruguay at 69.5 cents, close to the model's read — but the two pull apart on goals. The model sees over 2.5 at just 35%; the market splits it 50/50. Both-teams-to-score tells the same story, 29% from the model against 45% priced in. So the honest open question here isn't who wins, but whether Cape Verde find the net at all — and the model is markedly more sceptical than the traders.

Egypt arrive in Vancouver as clear favourites on paper — ranked 29th to New Zealand's 85th, with a squad valued at €116.48m against the All Whites' €34.35m — but this profiles as a tight, low-scoring affair rather than a procession. The model gives Egypt a 0.84-1.25 expected-goals edge and a 45.2% win chance, against 24.3% for New Zealand and a hefty 30.5% for the draw. That caution is everywhere in the scoreline: over 2.5 goals lands at just 34.7%, both teams scoring at 41.2%, and the three likeliest results are 0-1, 1-1 and 0-0. New Zealand's form does little to argue otherwise, with eight defeats in their last ten and recent maulings by Haiti and Australia.

Where it gets interesting is the gap to Polymarket. The market is firmly more bullish on Egypt, pricing them at 54.5 cents to the model's 45.2%, and trims the draw to 25.5 cents against the model's 30.5%. The market also leans slightly higher on goals, with over 2.5 at 42 cents versus the model's 34.7%. So the genuine open question is whether Egypt's quality simply overruns a fragile New Zealand, as the market expects, or whether this stays the cagey, draw-friendly contest the model sees.

Argentina arrive ranked third in the world with a squad valued at €782.5m against Austria's €242.2m, and the model treats this as exactly the mismatch the numbers suggest. It hands the hosts a 1.88 to 0.51 expected-goals edge and a 70.1% win probability, with Austria down at 8.8% and the draw at 21.2%. Argentina are favoured to open the scoring 71.1% of the time, and the most likely outcomes are all clean sheets — 1-0 at 16.8%, 2-0 at 16.2%, 3-0 at 10.2% — so this projects as a controlled, low-event win rather than a shootout. Tellingly, both teams to score sits at just 34.3%, reflecting how little the model expects from an Austrian side that has nonetheless quietly won eight of its last ten.

The market is more cautious about the favourite. Polymarket prices Argentina at 61.5 cents against the model's 70%, and gives Austria nearly double the model's chance, 16.5 cents to 8.8%. The same split shows on both-teams-to-score, 47.5 cents versus 34.3%, suggesting traders rate Austria's defensive names and recent form more highly than the goals model does. The totals barely differ — over 2.5 sits at 42.7% to the market's 48% — so the genuine open question is simply how much resistance Austria can muster.

This is a heavyweight mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. France, ranked first in the world with a squad valued at €1.52bn against Iraq's €21.2m, are projected for 2.51 expected goals to Iraq's 0.40, and that gap drives a moneyline of 83.2% for the home win against just 3.9% for Iraq, with the draw at 13%. The scoreline distribution is stacked with clean sheets: 2-0 (17.2%), 3-0 (14.4%) and 1-0 (13.5%) head the list, France are 81.4% to score first, and both teams to score sits at only 30.4%. Iraq arrive in decent form — a 1-1 draw at Spain and a play-off win over Bolivia — but the numbers say their job here is damage limitation.

The market broadly agrees but leans heavier in two places. Polymarket prices France at 87.5 cents to win, a touch firmer than the model's 83.2%, and trims the draw to 9.5. The clearer split is on goals: the market has over 2.5 at 65 cents and over 3.5 at 41.5, against the model's 55.5% and 33.2%. So the open question isn't who wins, but how many France pour in — and there the market expects a heavier French scoreline than the model does.

This is the matchup the model can't separate. Senegal arrive ranked 14th in the world to Norway's 31st, and the expected goals back that edge ever so slightly: 1.21 for Senegal against 1.13 for Norway, a margin small enough that the moneyline splits almost evenly — 37.2 percent for Senegal, 33.4 for Norway, with a thick 29.4 percent draw reflecting how tight this projects. Norway come in on a strong run, including 4-1 wins over Italy and Estonia in qualifying, while Senegal's recent record is more mixed, an AFCON campaign followed by a 3-2 friendly loss to the USA. The model reads this as a coin-flip leaning marginally to the more highly ranked side, with low scoring: only 41 percent of outcomes clear 2.5 goals, and 1-1 tops the scoreline chart at 13.8 percent.

The market sees it very differently, and the gap is the story. Polymarket prices Norway as clear favourites at 45 cents, with Senegal down at just 28.5 — almost the mirror image of the model, which makes Senegal the slight pick. The market also expects more goals, pricing both teams to score at 55.5 percent against the model's 48.2. That's a genuine open question worth watching, not a settled one.

This is a clear mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. Algeria sit 28th in the world to Jordan's 63rd, carry a squad valued at €256.9m against €20.3m, and arrive off a 1-0 win away to the Netherlands. The numbers reflect that gap: 2.36 expected goals for Algeria against just 0.72 for Jordan, a 73.7% win probability, and a 72.9% chance of striking first. The likeliest scorelines all run through an Algerian win — 0-2 (12.8%), 0-1 (10.5%) and 0-3 (10.1%) head the board — and Jordan's recent form, including a 1-4 loss to Switzerland and a 0-2 defeat to Colombia, does little to argue otherwise.

The market broadly agrees but is more cautious about the favourite. Polymarket prices Algeria at 65.5 cents to the model's 73.7%, while giving Jordan (14.5c) and the draw (20.5c) more room than the model's 9.2% and 17.2%. The other notable split is on goals: the model sees over 2.5 as a near-coin-flip at 59.4%, where the market sits at 48.5%, leaning under. On both questions the disagreement is real rather than decisive — the model expects a comfortable, fairly open Algeria win; the market hedges slightly toward caution and a tighter scoreline.

This is a heavyweight against a clear underdog: Portugal sit fifth in the world with a squad valued at €1.01bn, Uzbekistan fiftieth and worth €85m. The model frames it accordingly, projecting 1.8 expected goals for Portugal against just 0.58, and making them 66% to win with the draw a live 23% and an Uzbek upset only 11%. The shape is a Portugal side expected to control and score first 68% of the time, but not to run riot — the single most likely scoreline is a tight 1-0 at 16%, with 2-0 close behind, and a goalless draw still carries a 10% tag. Both teams scoring is below even money at 37%.

Where the model and the market part ways is on conviction. Polymarket prices Portugal at 79.5 cents to win, fully thirteen points clear of the model's read, and trims the draw to 14.5 cents. The market also expects more goals: over 2.5 trades at 61 cents against the model's 43%, and over 1.5 at 82 versus 69%. So the question isn't who wins but by how much. The model sees a businesslike, low-scoring Portugal job; the market is betting on a more emphatic one.

This is a heavy mismatch on paper, and the model treats it as one. England sit fourth in the world and arrive on a strong run — seven wins in their last ten, including 2-0 qualifying defeats of Serbia and Albania — while Ghana, ranked 74th, have lost five of their last ten and shipped goals freely against European opposition, 5-1 to Austria and 2-1 to Germany.
